solver_view.stem: A 10-cell battery module is tested under two electrical configurations. In the series configuration, thermal runaway (TR) triggered in one cell does not propagate. In the parallel configuration, TR propagates fiercely, with simulation indicating approximately 10°C higher peak temperature attributable to electrical energy transfer from healthy cells into the TR cell. In the parallel module, cells at lower SOC at the time of TR exhibit the longest propagation delay. An engineer is evaluating configuration-dependent TR propagation risk.
options: A. The higher temperature in the parallel module is primarily caused by greater thermal conductivity between cells in the parallel configuration rather than electrical energy redistribution.

B. In the parallel configuration, healthy cells actively contribute energy to the TR event, which is a counterintuitive mechanism that accelerates propagation beyond what thermal conduction alone would produce.
C. The propagation delay observed in lower-SOC cells within the parallel module is consistent with lower available electrochemical energy reducing the driving force for TR progression in those cells.
D. The series configuration's resistance to propagation implies that eliminating electrical interconnection between cells is a sufficient condition to prevent TR propagation in any module geometry.

E. The approximately 10°C temperature elevation from electrical energy transfer in the parallel module represents the total additional thermal load, so thermal management strategies need only account for this increment above the series baseline.

F. Parallel electrical configuration introduces an additional TR propagation pathway through electrical energy redistribution that is absent in series configuration, making configuration a critical design variable for propagation risk.
G. Because TR propagation did not occur in the series module, the series configuration can be considered thermally safe regardless of cell spacing or interstitial material properties.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly attributes the temperature elevation to electrical energy transfer from healthy cells, not to differential thermal conductivity between configurations.
option_judgments.error_type: mechanism substitution; ignores stated causal evidence

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows healthy cells release electrical energy into the TR cell in parallel configuration; this is counterintuitive and mechanistically supported as an accelerating pathway.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states lowest-SOC cell has longest propagation time due to lowest available energy, supporting this SOC-to-propagation-delay reasoning chain.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows series configuration prevented propagation in this specific test; generalizing to sufficiency under any geometry is an unsupported scope expansion.
option_judgments.error_type: overgeneralization from specific test condition to universal sufficiency

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The 10°C figure represents the electrical contribution increment; treating it as the total additional thermal load ignores other thermal pathways and is a measurement scope overclaim.
option_judgments.error_type: partial measurement treated as total system thermal load

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows parallel configuration introduces electrical short-circuit pathway absent in series, creating an additional propagation mechanism; this is a supported design-variable conclusion.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence supports series configuration preventing propagation in this test but does not establish thermal safety independent of cell spacing or interstitial materials; this is an unsupported system-level generalization.
option_judgments.error_type: system-level safety claim from single configuration test; ignores geometry and material variables

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism-based admissibility of conclusions about electrical configuration effects on TR propagation pathway and severity
analysis.primary_boundary: Parallel configuration introduces an electrical energy redistribution pathway that is distinct from and additive to thermal conduction; series configuration result cannot be universally generalized
intended_error_modes: Substituting thermal conductivity difference for electrical energy transfer mechanism
Treating 10°C increment as total thermal load
Overgeneralizing series safety to all geometries
Claiming series configuration is universally sufficient without material/spacing qualification
disallowed_extrapolations: Series configuration is sufficient for TR safety under any geometry
10°C increment represents total additional thermal management requirement
Thermal conductivity differences between configurations explain temperature elevation

solver_view.stem: A 100% SOC NCM 811 cylindrical cell with an intact casing undergoes thermal runaway. At the safety valve burst temperature (405.5 K), internal pressure from redox-generated gas is approximately three times the electrolyte vapor pressure. As temperature rises further toward the separator collapsing temperature, the ratio of redox-gas pressure to electrolyte vapor pressure increases to over six. The temperature-to-gas-generation-rate relationship is nonlinear across this range but becomes approximately linear near the separator collapsing temperature. Which of the following conclusions are supported?
options: A. Redox-generated gas pressure is the dominant contributor to safety valve bursting, not electrolyte vapor pressure, at the venting temperature.

B. Because the temperature–gas generation rate relationship is nonlinear before the separator collapsing temperature, a single-step reaction model is sufficient to describe internal pressure buildup in this regime.

C. Setting the safety valve burst threshold substantially above the manufacturer's specified range increases the risk of rupture and explosion because gas generated during thermal runaway cannot be vented in time.

D. The approximately linear relationship between temperature rate and gas generation rate near the separator collapsing temperature implies that a single dominant reaction controls pressure buildup at that stage.
E. At the venting temperature, electrolyte vapor pressure contributes meaningfully to total internal pressure but is not the primary driver of valve bursting.

F. The nonlinear temperature–gas generation relationship before the separator collapsing temperature indicates that total pressure at any intermediate temperature can be accurately predicted from a linear extrapolation of the venting-temperature values.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At venting temperature, Pg (995.79 kPa) is nearly three times PELE (302.42 kPa); redox gas bears main responsibility for valve bursting.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Nonlinearity before Tsc explicitly indicates multi-step reactions; a single-step model is contradicted by the evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that a high valve threshold prevents timely venting, causing rupture and explosion especially at high temperatures.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Approximate linearity near Tsc is described as an emergent behavior of multi-step reactions converging; it does not imply a single dominant reaction.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: PELE at venting is 302.42 kPa, contributing to total 1298.21 kPa; meaningful but not primary relative to Pg of 995.79 kPa.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Nonlinearity before Tsc explicitly invalidates linear extrapolation from venting-temperature values to intermediate temperatures.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Pressure contribution hierarchy at venting and the multi-step reaction interpretation of nonlinear temperature–gas rate relationship
analysis.primary_boundary: Approximate linearity near Tsc reflects multi-step convergence, not single-step dominance; PELE is real but secondary
intended_error_modes: Interpreting near-Tsc linearity as evidence of single-step reaction
Treating PELE as negligible rather than secondary
Assuming linear extrapolation is valid across the nonlinear pre-Tsc regime
disallowed_extrapolations: Single-step model adequacy from near-Tsc linearity
Linear pressure prediction across the full Tonset-to-Tsc range
High valve threshold improves safety by containing gas longer

solver_view.stem: A 1×5 NCM622 cell cluster is operated at varying states of charge. Experimental records show that reducing SOC from 100% to 50% decreases total gas generation from approximately 17.5 mol to 6.2 mol during thermal runaway propagation. At SOC below 25%, no thermal runaway propagation occurs within the cluster. A separate 1×4 cluster test with composite phase-change insulating material between cells achieved complete inhibition of thermal diffusion between cells. An engineer is asked to evaluate design and operational strategies for a module that must operate between 30% and 80% SOC. Which of the following conclusions are admissible?
options: A. Limiting operating SOC to below 25% would reliably prevent thermal runaway propagation in this cluster configuration, but this constraint falls outside the required 30–80% operating range and therefore cannot be applied here.

B. Within the 30–80% SOC operating range, reducing SOC toward the lower bound reduces gas generation during thermal runaway and therefore reduces propagation hazard, even though propagation cannot be fully eliminated by SOC control alone at these levels.

C. The complete inhibition of thermal diffusion achieved with composite phase-change insulating material in a 1×4 cluster is directly transferable to the 1×5 cluster at 80% SOC, eliminating the need for SOC-based operational limits.
D. Because gas generation decreases substantially between 100% and 50% SOC, the relationship between SOC and gas generation is confirmed to be linear across the full SOC range from 0% to 100%.

E. Composite phase-change insulating material represents a more reliable propagation barrier than SOC reduction alone for operation within the 30–80% SOC range, given that SOC reduction within this range cannot achieve the zero-propagation threshold.

F. The zero-propagation threshold at below 25% SOC applies specifically to the tested cluster geometry and chemistry; applying it as a universal design rule for all NCM module configurations is not supported by the evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence supports that below 25% SOC, no TR propagation occurs in this cluster. The stem defines a 30–80% operating range, placing the threshold outside the operational window. This is a direct admissible conclusion combining the threshold evidence with the stated constraint.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Gas generation decreases substantially from 100% to 50% SOC, indicating that lower SOC within the operating range reduces hazard even without eliminating propagation. This is an admissible directional inference that avoids overclaiming complete prevention.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The complete inhibition result was demonstrated in a 1×4 cluster. Directly transferring this to a 1×5 cluster at 80% SOC and claiming it eliminates the need for SOC limits is an inadmissible scale and condition transfer without supporting evidence.
option_judgments.error_type: scale transfer beyond evidence and unsupported elimination of operational constraint

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence provides two data points (100% and 50% SOC) showing a decrease in gas generation. Inferring a linear relationship across the full 0–100% SOC range from two points is an inadmissible extrapolation of functional form.
option_judgments.error_type: unjustified extrapolation of functional form from limited data points

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Since SOC reduction within 30–80% cannot reach the zero-propagation threshold, and composite phase-change material achieved complete thermal diffusion inhibition in a cluster test, the insulation material provides a more reliable propagation barrier within the constrained operating range. This is a multi-step admissible comparison.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The 25% SOC threshold was established for a 1×5 NCM622 cluster under specific test conditions. Applying it as a universal design rule for all NCM module configurations exceeds the evidence scope. This is an admissible boundary-of-evidence conclusion.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of SOC-based and insulation-based propagation control strategies under a defined operating range constraint
analysis.primary_boundary: The zero-propagation SOC threshold is cluster- and chemistry-specific; composite insulation results from one cluster size cannot be directly transferred to another without additional evidence
intended_error_modes: scale transfer of insulation result to different cluster size
linear extrapolation from two data points
universal application of a geometry-specific threshold
disallowed_extrapolations: Linear SOC–gas generation relationship across full range
Direct transferability of 1×4 insulation result to 1×5 cluster at high SOC

solver_view.stem: A 2.5 Ah NMC pouch cell undergoes thermal runaway triggered by overheating. At 30% SOC, negligible gas is released and no CO2 is detected. At 100% SOC, gas volume exceeds 6 L and peak reaction temperature surpasses 529 K. An engineer argues that reducing SOC to 30% is sufficient to eliminate explosion hazard. Which statements are admissible given this evidence?
options: A. At 30% SOC, the near-zero gas release and absence of CO2 indicate an incomplete reaction, which mechanistically limits the formation of flammable gas concentrations above the lower explosion limit.
B. At 100% SOC, the gas mixture lower explosion limit of 7 vol% combined with measured CO concentrations above that threshold confirms that ignitable conditions are achievable.
C. Reducing SOC to 30% eliminates thermal runaway risk entirely, since gas emission is negligible and no combustion-relevant species are detectable.
D. The higher peak reaction temperature at 100% SOC is solely caused by increased gas generation, and reducing gas volume would proportionally reduce peak temperature.
E. Because CO2 is absent at 30% SOC, the reaction completeness is lower, but this does not guarantee that all flammable species concentrations fall below their individual explosion limits.
F. The inverse relationship between SOC and reaction completeness means that a cell at 50% SOC will produce exactly half the gas of a cell at 100% SOC.
G. The non-linear increase in gas volume between 50% SOC (1.9 L) and 100% SOC (6.5 L) suggests a threshold-dependent reaction pathway becomes active above 50% SOC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Incomplete reaction at low SOC mechanistically links to reduced flammable species generation, consistent with C002 and C010.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Measured CO at 13.6 vol% exceeds the 7 vol% LEL derived from Le Chatelier's law, confirming ignitable conditions are achievable.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Negligible gas does not eliminate thermal runaway risk; it reduces explosion hazard but thermal runaway can still occur at low SOC.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Higher Treac at 100% SOC is attributed to additional stored electrochemical energy, not solely to gas generation volume.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Absence of CO2 indicates incomplete reaction but does not certify all flammable species are below LEL; admissible cautionary inference.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Gas volume at 50% SOC is 1.9 L vs 6.5 L at 100%; the ratio exceeds 3x, contradicting a linear halving assumption.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: More than threefold increase from 50% to 100% SOC despite only doubling SOC indicates a non-linear, threshold-sensitive pathway.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of explosion-hazard elimination claim and mechanism linking SOC to reaction completeness and gas flammability
analysis.primary_boundary: Negligible gas emission reduces but does not eliminate hazard; reaction incompleteness at low SOC does not certify sub-LEL concentrations for all species
intended_error_modes: unjustified linear scaling of gas volume with SOC
disallowed_extrapolations: SOC reduction eliminates thermal runaway
gas volume scales linearly with SOC
peak temperature is solely a function of gas volume

solver_view.stem: A 21,700 NMC811 cylindrical cell is subjected to an EV-ARC test. The following sequence is observed: self-heating begins near 65°C; at approximately 105°C the temperature rise rate increases sharply and consistently, marking a transition to accelerated heat release; at 143°C the temperature rise rate reaches 1°C/s; the bottom vent ruptures mechanically when internal gas pressure reaches 2 MPa; the gas vented includes hydrogen (~25%), carbon dioxide (~38%), carbon monoxide (~16%), methane (~18%), and ethane (~3%); the maximum temperature reaches 728°C at a peak rate of 7688°C/min. A safety engineer is asked to evaluate which temperature threshold is most appropriate to use as the intervention trigger for a battery management system designed to prevent full thermal runaway propagation in a module.
options: A. The temperature at which self-heating first becomes detectable (~65°C) is the most conservative and therefore most appropriate intervention trigger, because any earlier intervention is always preferable for safety.

B. The threshold at which the temperature rise rate first increases sharply and consistently (~105°C) represents the last point before the system departs from quasi-equilibrium, making it a more mechanistically justified intervention trigger than the 1°C/s threshold.

C. The 1°C/s threshold temperature (~143°C) is the most appropriate intervention trigger because it is the conventional definition of thermal runaway onset and represents a well-defined, measurable transition.

D. Once the temperature rise rate reaches 1°C/s at ~143°C, the subsequent progression to 728°C at 7688°C/min means that any intervention triggered at or after this point must account for the extremely limited time available before peak temperature is reached.

E. The mechanical vent rupture at 2 MPa internal pressure is a reliable early-warning signal that precedes the 1°C/s thermal runaway trigger temperature, and therefore vent rupture detection should replace temperature-based triggering.

F. The gas composition vented during thermal runaway—dominated by carbon dioxide and containing significant hydrogen and carbon monoxide—indicates that both combustion-supporting and flammable species are present, which is relevant to propagation hazard assessment at the module level.

G. Because the maximum temperature (728°C) is more than five times the self-heating onset temperature (65°C in Celsius), the energy released after the 1°C/s trigger point is negligible compared to total stored energy, and intervention at 143°C will reliably prevent significant temperature rise.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: While appearing conservative, triggering intervention at the self-heating onset (~65°C) is not necessarily most appropriate because self-heating at this stage is stable and the system is still in quasi-equilibrium. Triggering too early may cause unnecessary system shutdowns and does not reflect a mechanistically justified threshold. The claim that 'earlier is always preferable' is an unsupported absolute that ignores operational trade-offs.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The ~105°C threshold is explicitly defined as the departure from quasi-equilibrium, transitioning from stable self-heating to violently accelerated heat release. Intervening at this point—before the system enters the violent acceleration stage—is mechanistically more justified than waiting for the 1°C/s threshold, which occurs after the system has already departed from quasi-equilibrium.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: While 143°C is a conventional and well-defined threshold, the evidence shows that at this point the system has already transitioned to Stage III (violently accelerated heat release) and will reach 728°C at 7688°C/min. Describing it as 'most appropriate' without acknowledging the extremely limited intervention window after this point is an incomplete and potentially misleading safety judgment.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: At 7688°C/min, the time from 143°C to 728°C is extremely short (on the order of seconds to low minutes). Any intervention triggered at or after the 1°C/s threshold must account for this severely constrained response window, which is a critical engineering constraint for module-level propagation prevention.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence states vent rupture occurs when internal pressure reaches 2 MPa, but does not establish that this precedes the 1°C/s trigger temperature in the thermal runaway sequence. The temporal ordering of vent rupture relative to the 143°C trigger is not established by the evidence, making the claim that vent rupture detection should replace temperature-based triggering an unsupported recommendation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The gas composition includes flammable species (hydrogen ~25%, carbon monoxide ~16%, methane ~18%, ethane ~3%) alongside carbon dioxide (~38%). The co-presence of flammable gases and an oxidizer-supporting environment is directly relevant to propagation hazard at the module level, where vented gases from one cell can interact with neighboring cells or ignition sources.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The numerical comparison of temperatures in Celsius does not represent energy ratios. The claim that energy released after 143°C is negligible is not supported and is physically implausible given that the peak rate of 7688°C/min occurs after this point. This is a numerical reasoning error that misuses temperature values as energy proxies.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Selection of mechanistically justified intervention thresholds in a staged thermal runaway sequence, and the engineering implications of the extremely rapid progression after the 1°C/s trigger point
analysis.primary_boundary: The boundary between the quasi-equilibrium departure threshold (~105°C) and the conventional thermal runaway trigger (~143°C): the former is the last point before violent acceleration begins, the latter is already within the violent acceleration stage with severely limited intervention time
intended_error_modes: Treating earliest detectable threshold as automatically most appropriate without considering operational trade-offs
Accepting the conventional 1°C/s definition as 'most appropriate' without accounting for post-trigger dynamics
Assuming vent rupture precedes thermal runaway trigger without evidence of temporal ordering
Misusing Celsius temperature ratios as energy ratios
disallowed_extrapolations: Establishing temporal ordering of vent rupture relative to thermal runaway trigger temperature
Concluding that intervention at 143°C reliably prevents significant temperature rise given the post-trigger rate
Treating earlier intervention as universally preferable without operational context

solver_view.stem: A 21700 NMC cell at 100% SOC undergoes thermal runaway in an altitude-simulation chamber. As ambient pressure is reduced from 101 kPa to 30 kPa, the following observations are recorded: the onset temperature for thermal runaway increases, the time interval between safety venting and thermal runaway onset shortens from 48 s to 22 s, and the maximum jet flame height is non-monotonic with pressure, peaking at an intermediate pressure rather than at the lowest tested pressure. Mass loss across all tested pressures shows no consistent directional trend. A safety engineer is evaluating whether reduced ambient pressure at high altitude represents a more or less severe thermal runaway hazard compared to sea level. Which of the following statements are admissible based on this evidence?
options: A. The shortened venting-to-runaway interval at lower pressure means that any thermal management response triggered by venting detection has less time to act before runaway onset, increasing operational risk.
B. Because the onset temperature for thermal runaway increases at lower pressure, high-altitude operation is categorically safer than sea-level operation for this cell chemistry.

C. The non-monotonic behavior of jet flame height with pressure indicates that the relationship between ambient pressure and combustion behavior cannot be described by a simple monotonic model over the tested range.
D. The absence of a consistent trend in mass loss across pressures implies that the total energy content released during thermal runaway is strongly pressure-dependent.

E. The rate of temperature rise during thermal runaway is influenced by both internal battery pressure dynamics and external ambient pressure, so neither factor alone fully characterizes the severity of runaway at altitude.
F. Flame lift-off was not observed at any tested pressure, which confirms that jet fire hazard is negligible at reduced ambient pressure.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The venting-to-runaway interval decreases from 48 s to 22 s as pressure drops. This is a direct operational implication: safety systems triggered by venting detection have a shorter intervention window at altitude, which increases risk regardless of the higher onset temperature.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: While onset temperature increases at lower pressure, the venting-to-runaway interval is also shorter, meaning the window for intervention is reduced. A higher onset temperature does not categorically make operation safer when the dynamics after venting are more compressed. This is a conservative-sounding but incorrect conclusion that ignores the competing hazard factor.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Jet flame height peaks at 50 kPa rather than at the lowest (30 kPa) or highest (101 kPa) pressure, demonstrating non-monotonic behavior. This directly supports the conclusion that a simple monotonic model is insufficient to describe the pressure–combustion relationship over this range.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence states that environmental pressure has little significance on mass loss, with no consistent directional trend. The absence of a mass loss trend implies pressure has limited influence on total ejected mass, not that energy release is strongly pressure-dependent. This inverts the correct inference.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states that dT/dt during thermal runaway is related to both internal battery pressure and external environmental pressure, so neither alone fully determines runaway severity. This is a direct admissible inference from the heat transfer model evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The absence of flame lift-off does not imply jet fire hazard is negligible. Flame lift-off is one specific combustion behavior; the jet flame still reaches up to 0.28 m in height. Absence of lift-off is not equivalent to absence of hazard. This is a measurement scope distractor.
option_judgments.error_type: Measurement scope distractor; absence of one phenomenon ≠ absence of hazard

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-factor hazard assessment under reduced ambient pressure, requiring integration of onset temperature, venting interval, flame behavior, and mass loss evidence without single-variable overclaiming.
analysis.primary_boundary: A higher onset temperature at lower pressure does not categorically reduce hazard because the venting-to-runaway interval simultaneously decreases, and dT/dt depends on both internal and external pressure.
intended_error_modes: Single-variable safety conclusion ignoring competing factor
Inverting a non-result (no mass loss trend) into a strong causal claim
Treating absence of one combustion feature as evidence of negligible hazard
disallowed_extrapolations: Concluding categorical safety improvement from higher onset temperature alone
Inferring strong pressure dependence of energy release from the mass loss data
Treating flame lift-off absence as a proxy for overall fire hazard level

solver_view.stem: A 21700 NMC cell at 100% SOC undergoes thermal runaway testing across environmental pressures from 101 kPa to 30 kPa. Engineers observe that both the thermal runaway onset temperature and the maximum temperature increase as pressure decreases, while the time interval between safety venting and thermal runaway shortens from 48 s to 22 s. Which conclusions about the pressure-dependent hazard profile are admissible?
options: A. The shortened venting-to-runaway interval at low pressure indicates that despite a higher onset threshold, the effective warning time for intervention is reduced, increasing operational hazard.
B. Because onset temperature rises with decreasing pressure, cells operating at altitude face lower thermal runaway risk than at sea level.
C. The non-monotonic behavior of jet flame height, peaking at an intermediate pressure rather than the lowest tested, suggests that flame hazard cannot be ranked solely by ambient pressure.
D. The near-constant mass loss across all tested pressures implies that the total energy released during thermal runaway is pressure-independent and can be used as a fixed design parameter.
E. Flame lift-off observed at lower pressures for this cell format indicates a transition to a qualitatively different combustion regime below 50 kPa.

F. The simultaneous increase in both onset temperature and maximum temperature with decreasing pressure implies that the internal exothermic reaction kinetics are directly suppressed by reduced oxygen availability.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Higher onset temperature but halved venting-to-runaway interval means less usable warning time; hazard increases despite higher trigger threshold.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Higher onset temperature does not equal lower risk; shorter intervention window and higher Tmax at low pressure increase overall hazard.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Flame height peaks at 50 kPa, not 30 kPa, confirming non-monotonic pressure dependence; simple pressure-ranking of flame hazard is inadmissible.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Mass loss near-constancy does not equate to pressure-independent energy release; other thermal parameters vary significantly with pressure.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Flame lift-off was explicitly not observed at any tested pressure for this cell format, directly contradicting the option.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows pressure affects thermal runaway parameters but does not support attributing the mechanism to oxygen suppression of internal reactions.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-parameter hazard assessment under varying ambient pressure with non-monotonic flame behavior
analysis.primary_boundary: Higher onset temperature at low pressure does not imply lower hazard when intervention window shrinks and flame height is non-monotonic
intended_error_modes: scope expansion from mass loss to energy release
disallowed_extrapolations: Oxygen suppression mechanism for internal reactions
Monotonic pressure-hazard ranking
Mass loss as energy release proxy

solver_view.stem: A 300 Ah LFP cell at 100% SOC undergoes thermal runaway triggered by external heating. Surface temperature reaches 593°C. A technician concludes that because flaming combustion was present, it substantially elevated the peak surface temperature compared to a non-ignited test, and that the high surface temperature directly indicates severe internal exothermic reaction comparable to NMC chemistry. Which assessments are admissible given the described conditions?
options: A. The presence of flaming combustion accelerated the onset of thermal runaway but had limited influence on the peak surface temperature value.
B. The 593°C peak surface temperature confirms that the internal temperature also reached approximately 593°C, validating the severity of the internal reaction.

C. The LFP cell's failure to self-ignite under surface temperatures exceeding 600°C in preliminary tests suggests LFP chemistry has a higher resistance to self-sustaining combustion than implied by surface temperature alone.
D. Because mass loss after thermal runaway is independent of ignition state, the presence or absence of flaming combustion can be used to predict total mass ejected from the cell.
E. The peak surface temperature of 593°C at 100% SOC is consistent with an SOC-dependent trend, where lower SOC cells would exhibit lower peak surface temperatures under equivalent heating.
F. The peak surface temperature is primarily controlled by the combustion heat release rate, so suppressing ignition would reduce peak surface temperature proportionally.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly supports that flame accelerates TR onset but has little influence on peak surface temperature value.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Surface temperature cannot be equated to internal temperature; evidence shows internal temperatures can exceed 900°C while surface measurements differ substantially.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows LFP did not self-ignite even above 600°C surface temperature, indicating surface temperature alone is not sufficient to predict self-ignition behavior.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows mass loss is independent of ignition state, but this cannot be inverted to use ignition as a predictor of mass loss; causal direction is reversed.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows peak surface temperatures of 593.7, 435.9, and 294.3°C at 100%, 50%, and 20% SOC respectively, confirming the SOC-dependent trend.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows ignition state does not substantially alter peak surface temperature, contradicting the claim that suppressing ignition proportionally reduces peak temperature.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing the role of flaming combustion on TR onset versus peak surface temperature, and the admissibility of surface-to-internal temperature inference.
analysis.primary_boundary: Flame accelerates TR onset but does not substantially alter peak surface temperature; surface temperature cannot be equated to internal temperature.
intended_error_modes: measurement scope violation: surface temperature as proxy for internal temperature
causal direction reversal: using mass loss independence to infer ignition predicts mass loss
mechanism substitution: attributing peak surface temperature to combustion heat release rate
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring internal temperature from surface temperature measurement
Using ignition state to predict mass ejection
Claiming proportional relationship between ignition suppression and peak surface temperature reduction

solver_view.stem: A 38 Ah NCM battery is characterized using both accelerating rate calorimetry (ARC) and differential scanning calorimetry (DSC) of extracted electrode materials. ARC measurements use surface-mounted temperature sensors. DSC analysis of the cathode material shows a structural phase transition at 300°C and a second phase transition at 400°C. Separate electrode-only DSC experiments show the cathode alone undergoes thermal runaway at 189°C and the anode alone at approximately 240°C. The total heat generation calculated from DSC material analysis is 371,087 J, while the total heat generation calculated from ARC surface temperature measurements is 314,296 J. The battery does not undergo thermal runaway at 0% or 25% SOC but does at SOC values above 35%. The self-heat rate threshold defining the thermal runaway triggering temperature is 10°C/min. An engineer is evaluating the heat source hierarchy and measurement limitations for this system. Which of the following conclusions are admissible?
options: A. The fact that either electrode alone can undergo thermal runaway without the other electrode present indicates that internal short circuit is not required as the primary initiating heat source for thermal runaway in this system.

B. The ARC-derived total heat generation being lower than the DSC-derived value is best explained by the ARC surface temperature underestimating the internal temperature, causing the ARC calculation to undercount heat released near the cell core.

C. The structural phase transition of the cathode material at 300°C is the event that defines the thermal runaway triggering temperature in ARC testing, since it represents the onset of irreversible cathode decomposition.

D. Because the SEI decomposition and regeneration occur simultaneously between 100°C and 270°C, a battery whose surface temperature is measured at 200°C during ARC testing has definitively broken the SEI decomposition–regeneration balance.

E. The SOC threshold above which thermal runaway occurs (35%) implies that the electrochemical energy stored above this threshold is necessary to sustain the exothermic reaction cascade, and batteries below this threshold cannot generate sufficient heat from electrode reactions alone.

F. The cathode phase transition from rhombohedral to spinel at 300°C and from LiM2O4-type spinel to Co3O4-type spinel at 400°C occur above the measured thermal runaway triggering temperature of 188°C, indicating these transitions are consequences of thermal runaway progression rather than its initiating cause.
G. The difference between DSC-derived and ARC-derived heat totals cannot be used to determine the exact internal temperature profile of the battery during thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states that because either electrode alone can undergo thermal runaway, internal short circuit is not the major heat source during thermal runaway. This is a direct admissible conclusion from the electrode-only DSC experiments.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that ARC measures surface temperature and the inner temperature may be higher, which explains why the ARC-derived heat calculation (314,296 J) is lower than the DSC value (371,087 J). The mechanism chain is: surface temperature < internal temperature → ARC underestimates temperature rise → ARC underestimates heat released. This is directly supported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The thermal runaway triggering temperature (TTR) is defined as the temperature at which self-heat rate reaches 10°C/min, measured at 188°C in ARC testing. The cathode phase transition at 300°C occurs well above this TTR. Attributing TTR to the 300°C phase transition is a factual contradiction with the stated TTR definition and measured value.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that SEI decomposition and regeneration occur simultaneously between 100°C and 270°C, and the balance is not broken until temperature rises to 270°C or higher. A surface temperature of 200°C does not confirm that the internal temperature has reached 270°C, because ARC measures surface temperature and internal temperature may be higher. The surface measurement cannot definitively establish the internal thermal state. This option violates the measurement scope limitation explicitly stated in the evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence states that thermal runaway occurs above 35% SOC but does not provide a mechanistic explanation that the stored electrochemical energy above 35% is necessary to sustain the reaction cascade. The evidence also shows that electrode reactions alone (without ISC) can drive thermal runaway, and that electrode-only DSC experiments show thermal runaway. The claim that batteries below 35% SOC cannot generate sufficient heat from electrode reactions alone is an unsupported extrapolation beyond what the threshold observation establishes.
option_judgments.error_type: Unsupported mechanistic extrapolation from threshold observation

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: TTR is 188°C. The cathode phase transitions occur at 300°C and 400°C, both above TTR. Since thermal runaway is already triggered at 188°C, the phase transitions at higher temperatures occur during the progression of thermal runaway, not as its initiating cause. This is a valid multi-step temporal reasoning conclusion.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence explains the heat total discrepancy as arising from surface vs. internal temperature measurement, but does not provide a method to reconstruct the exact internal temperature profile from this difference alone. The ARC-DSC heat difference is a scalar quantity that reflects a systematic underestimate, not a spatially resolved temperature profile. This is an admissible limitation statement.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Heat source hierarchy in thermal runaway, measurement scope limitations of ARC surface temperature, and temporal ordering of thermal events relative to triggering temperature
analysis.primary_boundary: TTR is defined by self-heat rate threshold at 188°C; cathode phase transitions at 300°C and 400°C are post-triggering events; ARC surface temperature cannot establish internal temperature state; SOC threshold observation does not mechanistically explain the below-threshold behavior
intended_error_modes: Confusing post-triggering cathode phase transitions with the triggering mechanism
Extrapolating surface ARC temperature to internal SEI balance state
Inferring mechanistic necessity from SOC threshold observation
Treating heat total discrepancy as sufficient to reconstruct internal temperature profile
disallowed_extrapolations: Attributing TTR to cathode structural transitions that occur above TTR
Concluding internal SEI balance is broken from surface temperature measurement alone
Claiming below-threshold SOC cells cannot generate sufficient heat from electrode reactions
Using ARC-DSC heat difference to determine exact internal temperature distribution

solver_view.stem: A 3×3 module of cylindrical lithium-ion cells undergoes impact-induced thermal runaway in the central cell. Thermography confirms the impacted cell's internal contents exceed 800°C and flame temperatures surpass 500°C. Thermocouples in interstitial spaces record a brief plateau near 34°C followed by a surge to 250°C, then decay. Post-event thermography shows all surrounding cells remain at room temperature. Which conclusions about heat transfer and propagation behavior are supported by these observations?
options: A. The 250°C interstitial temperature surge confirms that neighbouring cells experienced internal exothermic reactions during the event.
B. The plateau period in the interstitial temperature profile is consistent with a transient thermal equilibration before the main gas and flame release reaches the surrounding space.

C. The fact that neighbouring cells remained at room temperature despite the central cell exceeding 800°C indicates that the geometry of cylindrical cell packing limits the conductive heat pathway between cells.
D. Because flame temperature exceeded 500°C, thermal runaway propagation to adjacent cells is thermodynamically guaranteed if contact time is sufficient.
E. The interstitial temperature reaching 250°C is sufficient evidence to conclude that neighbouring cells were brought to a state of imminent thermal runaway.
F. The absence of thermal runaway in neighbouring cells despite extreme local temperatures is consistent with restricted contact area reducing the effective heat flux into adjacent cells.
G. The thermography measurement of internal battery content temperature above 800°C directly quantifies the peak cathode decomposition temperature during the event.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Neighbouring cells stayed at room temperature; the 250°C surge reflects interstitial gas/flame exposure, not internal cell reactions.
option_judgments.error_type: Measurement scope distractor — surface/interstitial signal misattributed to internal cell state

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: A 9-second plateau at 34°C before the surge is consistent with a delay before vented gases and flame reach interstitial sensors.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Cylindrical geometry produces limited cell-to-cell contact area; this geometric constraint is the stated mechanism retarding conductive heat transfer.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows no propagation occurred despite >500°C flames; thermodynamic possibility does not override the geometric heat-transfer barrier demonstrated.
option_judgments.error_type: Causal overclaim — ignores contact-area constraint that evidence identifies as the controlling factor

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermography confirmed neighbouring cells remained at room temperature; 250°C in the interstitial space does not indicate imminent cell-level thermal runaway.
option_judgments.error_type: Scope expansion — interstitial measurement incorrectly extrapolated to internal cell state

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly attributes non-propagation to limited contact surface area reducing heat transfer, consistent with the observed outcome.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermography measures surface/emitted radiation; it records battery content temperature but cannot isolate cathode decomposition temperature specifically.
option_judgments.error_type: Measurement scope distractor — thermography result overclaimed as component-specific calorimetry

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of propagation and heat-transfer conclusions from interstitial temperature and thermography data in a cylindrical cell module
analysis.primary_boundary: Interstitial and surface temperature signals cannot be directly mapped to internal cell state; non-propagation is attributable to geometric contact-area limitation, not thermodynamic impossibility
intended_error_modes: Misattributing interstitial temperature rise to neighbouring cell internal reactions
Treating flame temperature as sufficient condition for propagation regardless of contact geometry
Overclaiming thermography resolution to identify component-specific temperatures
disallowed_extrapolations: Concluding neighbouring cells were near thermal runaway from interstitial 250°C reading
Asserting propagation is guaranteed given sufficient contact time based solely on flame temperature

solver_view.stem: A 4 Ah lithium iron phosphate cell undergoes thermal runaway inside an 80 L enclosed chamber. Measurements confirm that the released gas explosion propagates at supersonic speed and produces an average maximum overpressure of 383 kPa in the closed configuration. An explosion vent is then installed on the chamber. Tests with varying vent sizes show that as the dimensionless venting coefficient Kv decreases from 74.26 to 6.19, both peak overpressure and deflagration index decrease substantially. However, a venting efficiency index that accounts for both overpressure reduction and vent area cost reaches a maximum at an intermediate Kv value of 8.25 before declining. An engineer evaluates whether increasing vent area indefinitely is the optimal design strategy. Which of the following conclusions are admissible?
options: A. Increasing vent area beyond the optimal Kv value of 8.25 continues to reduce overpressure but does so with diminishing returns in venting efficiency, making arbitrarily large vents suboptimal by the efficiency criterion.

B. The supersonic propagation speed measured in the closed chamber confirms that the explosion regime is detonation, which means the explosion vent must be designed to contain rather than relieve the pressure wave to be effective.

C. At small vent areas, the risk of secondary explosion may increase relative to intermediate vent sizes, because delayed vent opening allows greater accumulation of combustible gases that can mix with external air after venting.

D. Because the explosion vent reduced average maximum overpressure by over 93%, it can be concluded that the explosion vent eliminates the structural risk to the chamber under all TR gas release scenarios.

E. The optimal venting coefficient Kv = 8.25 represents a design point where the tradeoff between overpressure reduction and vent area cost is most favorable, but this optimum is specific to the tested configuration and may not transfer directly to chambers of different volume or cell capacity.

F. Reducing Kv from 74.26 to 6.19 produces a monotonic decrease in both peak overpressure and deflagration index, which implies that the minimum achievable overpressure is obtained at the smallest possible Kv value regardless of other design constraints.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The venting efficiency index Ks reaches a maximum at Kv = 8.25 and then decreases as vent area increases further. This means larger vents beyond this point reduce overpressure but at a cost that exceeds the efficiency benefit, making them suboptimal by this criterion. This is a direct implication of the non-monotonic Ks behavior.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: While the supersonic propagation confirms detonation, the evidence shows that explosion vents do effectively reduce overpressure by over 93% even under these conditions. The claim that vents must 'contain rather than relieve' detonation waves is not supported and contradicts the demonstrated effectiveness of relief venting.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that small vent areas cause delayed opening due to higher opening pressure, leading to prolonged accumulation and increased concentration of combustible gases. After venting, high-temperature unburned gases can mix with external air to form a combustible cloud risking secondary explosion. This is directly supported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The 93% reduction is a measured average across tested configurations, but the residual overpressure range (7.2–27 kPa) and variability across Kv values means structural risk is reduced, not eliminated. Claiming elimination of structural risk under 'all TR gas release scenarios' is an inadmissible overclaim beyond the tested conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The optimal Kv = 8.25 is identified within the specific 80 L chamber with a 4 Ah LFP cell. The evidence does not support transferring this specific optimum to different chamber volumes or cell capacities, making the qualification that it is configuration-specific an admissible and necessary caveat.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: While decreasing Kv does reduce peak overpressure and deflagration index monotonically, the venting efficiency index Ks is non-monotonic and peaks at Kv = 8.25. The statement that the minimum overpressure at the smallest Kv is optimal 'regardless of other design constraints' ignores the efficiency penalty and potential secondary explosion risk at very small Kv values.
option_judgments.error_type: ignoring boundary conditions / ignoring multi-factor interaction

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Optimal explosion vent design under TR gas explosion conditions, including the non-monotonic efficiency relationship and secondary explosion risk at small vent areas
analysis.primary_boundary: Venting efficiency is non-monotonic with vent area; overpressure reduction does not eliminate structural risk; optimal Kv is configuration-specific; small vent areas introduce secondary explosion risk
intended_error_modes: Claiming venting eliminates structural risk from measured average reduction
Assuming monotonic optimality of smallest Kv ignoring efficiency and secondary explosion
Misinterpreting detonation classification as requiring containment rather than relief
disallowed_extrapolations: Transferring optimal Kv to different chamber volumes or cell capacities
Claiming 93% reduction equates to elimination of structural risk
Applying detonation classification to conclude vents are ineffective

solver_view.stem: A 50 Ah prismatic LFP cell at 25°C is being overcharged under constant-current conditions. Surface infrared monitoring shows that below 100% SOC, the thermal pattern is a stable lateral stratification aligned with the tab region, with a maximum surface temperature difference of 18°C. As overcharging continues to 105% SOC, the thermal pattern transitions to a concentric distribution centered on the middle of the cell face, and this transition precedes any visible mechanical deformation by approximately 38 seconds. Beyond 120% SOC, the surface temperature rise rate increases sharply relative to the slow rate observed between 90% and 120% SOC. An engineer proposes using the thermal pattern transition at 105% SOC as the primary intervention trigger. Which of the following conclusions about this monitoring strategy and the underlying thermal behavior are supported by the described evidence?
options: A. The shift from lateral to concentric thermal distribution at 105% SOC provides a warning signal that precedes the onset of visible mechanical deformation, making it a structurally earlier indicator than naked-eye inspection of cell bulging.
B. Because the concentric hotspot pattern appears before visible deformation, it can be concluded that internal gas generation has not yet begun at the moment the thermal pattern changes.

C. The lateral thermal stratification below 100% SOC is attributable to current concentration effects near the tab geometry, and its disappearance at 105% SOC is consistent with a redistribution of internal heat sources away from the tab region.

D. The sharp increase in temperature rise rate above 120% SOC confirms that the thermal behavior between 90% and 120% SOC is governed by the same mechanism as that above 120% SOC, differing only in rate.

E. Using the thermal pattern transition as an intervention trigger is more conservative than waiting for the sharp temperature rise rate increase, since the pattern transition occurs at a lower SOC than the rate acceleration threshold.

F. The 38-second lead time over visible deformation is sufficient to guarantee that a discharge intervention initiated at the pattern transition will prevent irreversible capacity loss in all LFP cell formats.

G. The tab-aligned lateral stratification during normal charging and its replacement by a central hotspot at 105% SOC represent two distinct thermal regimes whose boundary is defined by the overcharge threshold, not by the absolute surface temperature value.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence states the concentric thermal field emerges 38 ± 5 s before mechanical deformation becomes visible, directly supporting the claim that it is an earlier indicator than naked-eye bulge detection.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence does not state that internal gas generation has not begun at the moment of thermal pattern transition. The 38-second lead over visible deformation does not imply absence of internal gas; it only establishes a temporal relationship with visible bulging. This is a measurement-scope overclaim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence attributes the tab-aligned lateral stratification to current concentration (skin effect) and describes its replacement by a concentric hotspot at 105% SOC, which is consistent with internal heat sources redistributing away from the tab region as overcharge chemistry begins to dominate.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence shows a sharp regime change above 120% SOC (rate jumps from ~0.013°C/s to 0.62°C/s), which is inconsistent with the same mechanism operating at different rates. The large rate discontinuity implies a different governing mechanism above 120% SOC, not a continuous acceleration of the same process.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The thermal pattern transition occurs at 105% SOC while the sharp rate increase occurs above 120% SOC. Triggering at the pattern transition therefore intervenes at a lower SOC, making it the more conservative (earlier) intervention point relative to the rate-based threshold.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence explicitly states that the 105% SOC threshold and associated thermal laws are specific to fresh 50 Ah prismatic LFP cells at 25°C, and that universality across different capacities, packaging forms, and conditions requires further investigation. Guaranteeing prevention across all LFP formats is an unsupported generalization.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence describes two distinct thermal patterns (lateral stratification below 100% SOC; concentric hotspot at 105% SOC) whose transition is linked to crossing the overcharge threshold, not to reaching a specific surface temperature. This supports the conclusion that the regime boundary is SOC-defined rather than temperature-defined.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of thermal pattern transition as an early warning trigger and the mechanistic interpretation of thermal regime changes during LFP overcharge
analysis.primary_boundary: Conclusions about internal state (gas generation, mechanism identity) cannot be drawn solely from surface thermal pattern observations; scope is also limited to the specific cell format and conditions tested
intended_error_modes: Measurement scope overclaim: inferring absence of internal gas from surface thermal pattern alone
Mechanism substitution: treating the slow and fast temperature rise regimes as the same mechanism at different rates
Unsupported generalization: extending the 105% SOC threshold to all LFP cell formats
disallowed_extrapolations: Internal gas generation state cannot be inferred from surface thermal pattern alone
The 105% SOC threshold cannot be generalized to other cell formats, capacities, or SOH states without further evidence
The slow and fast thermal rise regimes above and below 120% SOC cannot be assumed to share the same governing mechanism

solver_view.stem: A 53 Ah NCM pouch cell at 100% SOC is subjected separately to three thermal runaway triggers: nail penetration, lateral external heating, and overcharge at 1C. Measurements show that the internal temperature during nail penetration reaches approximately 1005 °C while the surface temperature peaks at 774 °C. Under lateral heating, the surface trigger temperature is 233 °C and thermal runaway spreads to the rear surface in approximately 20 s. Under overcharge, thermal runaway is triggered at a surface temperature of 117 °C after the cell has been charged to approximately 128% SOC. An engineer must assess which diagnostic and mechanistic conclusions are supported by these observations. Which of the following are admissible conclusions?
options: A. For nail penetration, surface temperature measurement alone is insufficient to characterize the peak thermal state of the cell interior during thermal runaway, because the internal temperature substantially exceeds the surface temperature.
B. The lower surface trigger temperature under overcharge compared to lateral heating indicates that overcharge generates less total heat than lateral heating before thermal runaway onset.
C. The longer injection duration under overcharge compared to nail penetration is consistent with a greater cumulative energy input to the cell before thermal runaway is triggered.
D. The 20-second propagation time across the cell thickness under lateral heating implies that the same propagation rate would be observed in a multi-cell module under identical heating conditions.

E. Under nail penetration, the fact that internal temperature rise precedes surface temperature rise indicates that heat originates internally and diffuses outward, rather than being imposed from the cell surface.
F. The surface trigger temperature under overcharge being lower than under lateral heating is sufficient to conclude that overcharge is the least hazardous of the three abuse modes tested.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence directly shows a ~231 °C gap between internal (1005 °C) and surface (774 °C) temperatures during nail penetration, establishing that surface measurement underestimates peak internal thermal state.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Trigger surface temperature reflects the condition at which thermal runaway is initiated, not the total heat generated before that point. Overcharge involves electrochemical energy accumulation over 1020 s to 128.3% SOC; the lower trigger temperature does not imply less total energy input. This is a mechanism confusion distractor.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence states that injection time is positively correlated with energy input before thermal runaway, and overcharge has the longest injection duration (29 s vs. 22 s for nail penetration), supporting the conclusion of greater cumulative energy input.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The 20-second propagation time is a cell-level measurement for a single pouch cell under lateral heating. Extrapolating this rate to a multi-cell module involves scale transfer beyond the evidence scope, as module-level propagation depends on inter-cell spacing, thermal conductivity, and configuration.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence explicitly states that internal temperature rise occurs much earlier than surface temperature rise after nail penetration, indicating internal heat generation diffusing outward—consistent with the described conclusion.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Overcharge produces the highest peak surface temperature (935 °C) and longest injection duration of the three modes, indicating greater overall energy release. The lower trigger temperature reflects only the initiation threshold, not overall hazard severity. This is a conservative-appearing but mechanistically incorrect conclusion.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Distinguishing trigger temperature, internal temperature, injection duration, and peak temperature as independent indicators of thermal runaway mechanism and severity across abuse modes
analysis.primary_boundary: Trigger surface temperature reflects initiation threshold, not total energy input or overall hazard; internal and surface temperatures are decoupled; cell-level propagation time cannot be directly transferred to module level
intended_error_modes: Equating low trigger temperature with low total energy input
Using trigger temperature as a proxy for overall hazard ranking
Extrapolating single-cell propagation time to module-level behavior
disallowed_extrapolations: Trigger temperature directly indicates total heat generated before thermal runaway
Cell-level lateral propagation time applies directly to multi-cell modules
Lower trigger temperature implies lower overall abuse hazard

solver_view.stem: A 9-cell battery module is assembled using cells with a high-safety electrolyte (HSE) that is non-flammable and raises the thermal runaway trigger temperature by ~24°C relative to base electrolyte (BE). Under a thermal abuse scenario initiating runaway in cell 1, only three cells trigger runaway with HSE versus full propagation through all nine cells with BE. The maximum module temperature with HSE reaches ~203°C in cell 4. Which conclusions are admissible regarding HSE module-level behavior?
options: A. HSE eliminates thermal runaway propagation entirely in the module because its non-flammability prevents heat transfer between cells.
B. HSE arrests propagation after three cells, indicating that the non-flammable electrolyte reduces but does not eliminate inter-cell thermal coupling under these conditions.
C. The ~24°C increase in trigger temperature is the sole mechanism responsible for propagation arrest, since flammability plays no independent role.

D. The maximum temperature of ~203°C in cell 4 with HSE suggests that cells beyond cell 3 received insufficient thermal input to reach their elevated trigger threshold, making propagation arrest a threshold-dependent outcome.
E. Because HSE cells still undergo thermal runaway in three cells, the electrolyte change provides no meaningful safety benefit at the module level.
F. The propagation behavior difference between BE and HSE cannot be attributed to electrolyte properties alone without ruling out differences in cell spacing or thermal management.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Three cells still trigger runaway with HSE; non-flammability delays but does not eliminate propagation, contradicting 'eliminates entirely'.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Three cells triggered versus nine confirms partial arrest; non-flammability reduces heat release, reducing but not eliminating inter-cell coupling.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Non-flammability independently reduces heat release and propagation energy; attributing arrest solely to the 24°C trigger shift ignores the flammability mechanism.
option_judgments.error_type: single-variable causal overclaim ignoring multi-factor interaction

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Cell 4 maximum of ~203°C is below HSE trigger threshold of ~248°C, consistent with propagation arrest being threshold-dependent.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Reduction from 9-cell full propagation to 3-cell partial propagation and 730°C to 203°C maximum temperature represents substantial module-level safety benefit.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: This is a conservative but unsupported restriction; the controlled comparison is between BE and HSE cells under the same module simulation conditions.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Module-level thermal runaway propagation arrest mechanism and admissible attribution to HSE properties
analysis.primary_boundary: Partial arrest (three cells) versus complete elimination of propagation; threshold-dependent versus mechanism-based arrest
intended_error_modes: Attributing arrest solely to trigger temperature shift while ignoring flammability contribution
Dismissing partial arrest as no safety benefit
disallowed_extrapolations: Concluding HSE eliminates all propagation risk
Claiming trigger temperature shift alone explains module-level outcome

solver_view.stem: A CFD model is used to predict explosion pressure in a 1-m channel filled with Li-ion vent gas. Experiments at equivalence ratio 1.08 yield a maximum pressure of 1.36 kPa (gauge). The CFD simulation predicts 1.45 kPa (gauge) at equivalence ratio 1.0. Separately, the simulation fails to reproduce pressure and flame front velocity oscillations observed as the flame approaches the channel opening. An engineer must judge which aspects of the simulation are validated and which are not.
options: A. The CFD model can be considered validated for peak explosion pressure prediction within the tested channel geometry, since predicted and measured peak pressures agree within approximately 7%.

B. Because the simulation underpredicts peak pressure relative to experiment at a slightly different equivalence ratio, the model systematically underestimates explosion hazard across all equivalence ratios.
C. The failure to reproduce oscillatory behavior near the channel opening means the simulation cannot be relied upon to predict dynamic pressure loading on structures located near the vent opening.
D. Agreement in peak pressure magnitude alone is sufficient to validate the model for predicting flame propagation dynamics throughout the entire channel.

E. The discrepancy in equivalence ratio between peak pressure predictions (1.0 vs. 1.08) indicates the model's stoichiometric sensitivity differs from the experiment, which is a limitation relevant to mixture composition uncertainty.
F. Since peak pressures differ by less than 10%, the oscillation prediction failure is inconsequential for any engineering application of this model.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 1.36 vs 1.45 kPa is approximately 7% difference; peak pressure agreement in the tested geometry supports limited validation for that quantity.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The simulation predicts higher pressure than experiment at a different equivalence ratio; this is not a systematic underprediction across all equivalence ratios.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Oscillations near the channel opening are not reproduced; dynamic pressure loads at that location cannot be reliably predicted by this model.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Peak pressure agreement does not validate flame propagation dynamics; oscillation failure explicitly shows dynamic behavior is not captured.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Peak pressure occurring at different equivalence ratios in simulation vs. experiment indicates differing stoichiometric sensitivity, relevant when mixture composition is uncertain.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Oscillation failure is consequential for applications requiring dynamic load prediction near the vent opening, regardless of peak pressure agreement magnitude.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Scope of CFD model validation: what is and is not supported by peak pressure agreement when dynamic behavior is not reproduced
analysis.primary_boundary: Peak pressure agreement validates only that quantity in that geometry; oscillation failure invalidates dynamic predictions near the opening
intended_error_modes: conflating peak pressure validation with full dynamic validation
overgeneralizing directional error across equivalence ratios
dismissing oscillation failure based on magnitude agreement
disallowed_extrapolations: systematic underprediction across all equivalence ratios
full flame dynamics validation from peak pressure alone
inconsequentiality of oscillation failure for all applications

solver_view.stem: A CNN-LSTM detection system monitors temperature contour images from a battery pack to identify abnormal heat generation. The system uses sequences of frames at a fixed time step; testing showed that increasing the time step from 8 s to 16 s per sequence reduced validation accuracy from approximately 97% to 89%, attributed to reduced contrast between risk and safe states and increased temperature change per step. The system achieves a recall of 100% and an inference time under 0.1 s per batch. A separate finding shows that when two cells undergo thermal runaway simultaneously rather than one, detection performance improves because the temperature contrast in the monitored domain becomes more prominent. An engineer proposes deploying this system as the primary safety response trigger for a pack, arguing that 100% recall and sub-0.1 s inference time together guarantee that no thermal runaway event will go undetected in time for intervention. Which of the following judgments about this proposal are correct?
options: A. The 100% recall result is specific to the experimental conditions under which the model was trained and evaluated; applying it as a guarantee across all deployment configurations requires assuming that operational temperature contrast patterns remain within the training distribution.

B. The improvement in detection performance when two cells are in thermal runaway rather than one is counterintuitive from a safety perspective, because the condition that improves detection is itself a more severe failure state.
C. The sub-0.1 s inference time is sufficient to guarantee timely intervention because it is negligible compared to the timescales of thermal runaway propagation in typical battery modules.

D. The degradation in accuracy when the time step is doubled suggests that the model's performance is sensitive to the temporal resolution of the input, which means detection reliability could degrade if the monitoring system's sampling rate changes in deployment.

E. Because recall is 100%, the F1-score of 85.02 must imply a precision below 100%, meaning the system produces false positives; deploying it as the sole trigger without false-positive filtering could cause unnecessary safety activations.

F. The detection system's performance characteristics are sufficient to conclude that it can replace electrochemical state monitoring because temperature contour imaging captures all relevant precursors to thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The 100% recall was achieved under specific experimental conditions. Generalizing it as a deployment guarantee requires that the operational environment produces temperature contrast patterns similar to the training data. This is a standard admissibility boundary for ML-based detection systems and is not contradicted by any evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence states that two TR cells produce better detection than one because the temperature contrast is more prominent. This is counterintuitive from a safety standpoint: the condition that makes detection easier (two cells in TR) is a more dangerous failure state than the single-cell case. This is a genuine counterintuitive but correct observation supported directly by the evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The inference time being under 0.1 s addresses computational latency only. Whether this is sufficient for timely intervention depends on the full response chain (detection → signal transmission → actuator response → physical intervention), none of which are addressed by inference time alone. The evidence does not support the claim that inference time alone guarantees timely intervention.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence directly shows that doubling the time step from 8 s to 16 s reduced validation accuracy from ~97% to ~89%. This demonstrates sensitivity to temporal resolution. If deployment sampling rate differs from training conditions, detection reliability could degrade by the same mechanism.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: F1-score is the harmonic mean of precision and recall. With recall at 100% and F1 at 85.02%, precision must be below 100% (approximately 73.9% by calculation from the harmonic mean formula). This means false positives exist. Deploying the system as a sole trigger without false-positive management would cause unnecessary activations. This is a correct multi-step inference from the reported metrics.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Temperature contour imaging captures thermal signatures but does not capture electrochemical state variables such as voltage, impedance, or SOC. The evidence does not claim that temperature imaging captures all relevant precursors to thermal runaway. This is an unsupported scope expansion.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of deployment guarantee claims for a CNN-LSTM detection system given known performance boundaries and metric relationships
analysis.primary_boundary: 100% recall under experimental conditions does not constitute a deployment guarantee; F1 below 100% with perfect recall implies false positives; inference time addresses only one component of intervention latency
intended_error_modes: Treating experimental recall as a universal deployment guarantee (engineer's proposal)
Inferring that sub-0.1 s inference time alone guarantees timely intervention (C)
Scope expansion from temperature imaging to full electrochemical state monitoring (F)
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming temperature contour imaging captures all thermal runaway precursors
Treating inference time as equivalent to total intervention response time
Generalizing 100% recall from training conditions to all deployment configurations

solver_view.stem: A CTP battery module undergoes thermal runaway in a closed nitrogen-filled enclosure instrumented with pressure and voltage sensors. Engineers observe that abrupt chamber pressure changes occur slightly before the recorded thermal runaway trigger times for each cell in the module. The vented gas composition is dominated by H2, CO2, and CO, with a calculated explosion limit substantially wider than methane and a lower explosive limit near 6%. Which conclusions about monitoring strategy and gas hazard are admissible given these conditions?
options: A. Pressure monitoring in the enclosed space provides earlier warning of thermal runaway onset than voltage monitoring alone.
B. The wide explosion limit range and low explosive limit of the vented gas indicate that the gas mixture poses a higher explosion risk upon air ingress than a pure methane leak of equivalent volume.
C. Because the enclosure is filled with nitrogen, the vented gas cannot form a flammable mixture and the explosion limit calculation is irrelevant to operational safety.
D. The dominance of H2 in the vented gas composition is consistent with conditions of advanced thermal runaway or deflagration rather than initial venting stages where CO2 typically dominates.
