option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows airflow is effective below 370 K for specific velocity but TR can still occur above 360 K at 5 C depending on channel thickness; universality is unsupported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The ~35 K difference is supported, but concluding channel geometry substitutes for active cooling under all conditions ignores the 425 K collapse threshold.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 16 mm channel threshold is 395 K while 2 mm is 360 K; between 395–425 K the 16 mm channel remains safe while 2 mm has already entered TR territory.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Regime-dependent effectiveness of airflow cooling and channel thickness on thermal runaway threshold under defined discharge conditions
analysis.primary_boundary: Airflow cooling effectiveness collapses above 425 K regardless of velocity or channel geometry
intended_error_modes: overgeneralization of airflow effectiveness across all temperatures
single-variable dominance claim for discharge rate
scope expansion claiming channel geometry replaces active cooling universally
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming airflow at any velocity prevents TR at all ambient temperatures
Concluding channel thickness effect dominates discharge rate universally
Extending 5 C results to all discharge rates without qualification

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion cell undergoes thermal runaway triggered by an electric heating plate in a compact stacked configuration. Observers note that no combustion occurs and that ejected material color lightens progressively during the event. A second identical cell undergoes thermal runaway in an open oven-heating configuration and combustion is observed. An engineer must explain the mechanistic difference and characterize the ejecta evolution. Which statements are admissible?
options: A. Combustion is absent in the compact-stack case because confinement suppresses the exothermic reactions within the cell, preventing heat generation entirely.

B. The absence of combustion in the compact-stack case is consistent with surrounding temperatures remaining below the ignition threshold of ejected combustible gases, due to restricted heat dissipation geometry.
C. The progressive lightening of ejecta color in the compact-stack case indicates that solid particle content in the ejecta decreases as thermal runaway advances.
D. The presence of combustion in the oven case but not the compact-stack case implies that the oven-heated cell generates more internal heat than the compact-stack cell.
E. The initial ejecta in the compact-stack thermal runaway event is dominated by gaseous species, with solid particle fraction increasing over time.
F. Compact stacking geometry affects the ignition behavior of ejected material without necessarily altering the internal electrochemical reactions driving thermal runaway.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Confinement restricts heat release to surroundings, not internal reactions; internal exothermic reactions still occur.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Compact stacking restrains heat release such that surrounding temperature stays below ignition threshold of combustibles.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lightening ejecta color directly indicates decreasing solid particle proportion in ejecta as thermal runaway progresses.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Combustion difference reflects ignition conditions in surroundings, not a difference in internal heat generation between cells.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states initial ejecta contains large solid particle quantities that decrease over time; this option reverses the trend.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Stacking geometry affects external ignition environment without evidence of altering internal electrochemical runaway reactions.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanistic distinction between internal thermal runaway reactions and external ignition conditions under geometric confinement
analysis.primary_boundary: Confinement suppresses ignition of surroundings by limiting heat dissipation, not by suppressing internal reactions
intended_error_modes: conflating external ignition suppression with internal reaction suppression
reversing solid particle fraction trend in ejecta
attributing combustion difference to internal heat generation difference
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring reduced internal heat generation from absence of external combustion
Inferring gaseous-dominated initial ejecta from color lightening trend

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion cell using an NMC622 cathode is charged to approximately 80% SOC at room temperature. The electrolyte contains ethylene carbonate (EC) and trace OH⁻ contamination. Engineers observe gas generation and debate its origin. Which conclusions about the gas evolution mechanism are admissible under these conditions?
options: A. Singlet oxygen generated from the NMC cathode surface at ~80% SOC can react with EC to produce CO2, CO, and H2O, making cathode-side oxidation a plausible contributor to gas generation.
B. OH⁻-driven EC hydrolysis can occur at room temperature and contributes more gas production than H2O-driven decomposition under these conditions, making OH⁻ contamination a significant gas source.
C. H2O-driven EC decomposition is the dominant gas source at room temperature because water is universally more reactive than hydroxide ions toward carbonate solvents.

D. C2H4 generation at room temperature is primarily attributed to EC reduction at the graphite anode under ~0.8 V, and this pathway is active only in water-free electrolyte, so trace OH⁻ does not enhance C2H4 production.
E. Because singlet oxygen from NMC requires overcharge beyond 4.5 V to escape the lattice, no cathode-derived oxygen species can contribute to gas generation at 80% SOC.

F. The simultaneous presence of OH⁻ and EC means that cathode-side and anode-side gas mechanisms can both be active, but their relative magnitudes cannot be ranked without further data on OH⁻ concentration.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Singlet oxygen from NMC at ~80% SOC reacting with EC to yield CO2, CO, H2O is directly supported by evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: OH⁻ is a stronger nucleophile than H2O and drives EC hydrolysis at room temperature; H2O-driven decomposition requires ≥40°C.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: H2O-driven EC decomposition is only relevant at ≥40°C; at room temperature OH⁻ dominates, making this claim a condition violation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: C2H4 from EC reduction at ~0.8 V is specific to water-free electrolyte; OH⁻ contamination does not enhance this anode pathway.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Oxygen evolution from NMC lattice requires >4.5 V, but singlet oxygen from Li2CO3 decomposition occurs at 3.8 V and at ~80% SOC.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Both mechanisms are active under stated conditions; ranking their magnitudes requires OH⁻ concentration data not provided in the stem.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-source gas generation mechanism admissibility at room temperature and ~80% SOC with EC electrolyte and OH⁻ contamination
analysis.primary_boundary: H2O-driven EC decomposition is only active at ≥40°C; OH⁻ dominates at room temperature; singlet oxygen from NMC surface is active at ~80% SOC without requiring >4.5 V overcharge
intended_error_modes: Confusing H2O vs OH⁻ reactivity regimes
Conflating Li2CO3 surface decomposition pathway with lattice oxygen evolution threshold
Assuming C2H4 pathway is enhanced by OH⁻
disallowed_extrapolations: Ranking cathode vs anode gas magnitude without concentration data
Extending lattice oxygen evolution threshold to surface singlet oxygen formation

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion cell with a graphite anode is subjected to accelerated thermal abuse starting from an ambient temperature of 25°C. The anode has a relatively large surface area due to fine particle size. The cell has undergone 45 charge/discharge cycles at 0°C and is at high SOC. An engineer is evaluating the sequence and character of thermal events as the cell heats up. Which of the following statements about the thermal runaway progression in this cell are admissible given the described conditions?
options: A. The heat released during SEI decomposition will be greater in this cell than in an otherwise identical cell with a coarser anode particle size at the same lithium intercalation level, because SEI heat release scales with anode surface area rather than with intercalated lithium content.

B. The first exothermic reactions attributable to cycling-induced degradation will initiate at a lower temperature than in a fresh cell at the same SOC, because accumulated cycling reduces the activation energy of those reactions.

C. Because the cell is at high SOC, the cathode will begin releasing oxygen at a lower temperature than it would at low SOC, making the onset of cathode-driven reactions occur earlier in the heating sequence.

D. The SEI decomposition peak near 100°C is the rate-limiting step that determines the maximum temperature the cell can reach during thermal runaway, since all subsequent reactions depend on SEI failure.

E. The hydrogen released during the early exothermic group is primarily a product of cathode oxygen release reacting with the electrolyte, since hydrogen-rich gases dominate the early gas composition.
F. The energy released by the early group of exothermic reactions in this 45-cycle cell is independent of the cell's SOC, even though the cell's overall thermal runaway temperature is SOC-dependent.
G. Electrolyte decomposition contributes negligibly to the final cell temperature because it occurs after cathode reactions have already consumed most available reactants.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states SEI heat release is independent of intercalated lithium amount but very sensitive to anode surface area. A finer particle size increases surface area, increasing meta-stable SEI and therefore heat release. This is a direct admissible inference.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that with increasing hydrogen accumulation in graphite from cycling, activation energy decreases and initiation temperature of the exothermic reaction decreases. After 45 cycles the first exothermic group initiates at approximately 32°C versus no such reactions at NC=0, confirming lower initiation temperature in aged cells.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that with SOC growth, cathode thermal stability falls and oxygen release from the cathode grows, shifting thermal runaway to lower temperatures. This supports the conclusion that at high SOC, cathode-driven reactions onset earlier.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: SEI decomposition is an early event in the sequence but the evidence describes subsequent reactions (anode-electrolyte at >120°C, cathode decomposition at ~170°C, electrolyte decomposition at >200°C with temperatures reaching over 600°C) that drive the maximum temperature far beyond what SEI decomposition alone could produce. SEI failure is a trigger, not the rate-limiting step for maximum temperature.
option_judgments.error_type: causal overclaim — single-step trigger misidentified as the dominant heat source

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states the gas from the first exothermic group is more than 99% hydrogen and that this group is associated with cycling-induced degradation of the graphite anode (hydrogen accumulation in graphite). The evidence does not attribute this hydrogen to cathode oxygen reactions; rather, the hydrogen is linked to anode-side processes. Attributing it to cathode oxygen release contradicts the evidence.
option_judgments.error_type: mechanism substitution — correct gas species attributed to wrong reaction pathway

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states that the temperature ranges and energy of the first group of exothermic reactions do not depend on SOC but depend heavily on the number of charge/discharge cycles. Simultaneously, the overall thermal runaway temperature is SOC-dependent via cathode stability. These two mechanisms operate independently, making this a counterintuitive but evidence-supported conclusion.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that electrolyte decomposition combined with heat from combustion with released oxygen can elevate temperatures to over 600°C, indicating it is a major contributor to maximum cell temperature, not negligible.
option_judgments.error_type: scope reversal — secondary framing applied to a dominant heat contributor

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-stage thermal runaway sequence with SOC-dependent and cycle-dependent mechanisms operating independently on different reaction groups
analysis.primary_boundary: The first exothermic group energy is cycle-dependent but SOC-independent, while overall thermal runaway temperature is SOC-dependent — these two dependencies must not be conflated
intended_error_modes: causal overclaim treating SEI as rate-limiting for maximum temperature
mechanism substitution attributing anode-side hydrogen to cathode oxygen reactions
scope reversal minimizing electrolyte decomposition contribution
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring that SOC independence of the first reaction group implies SOC independence of the full thermal runaway sequence
Inferring that SEI decomposition temperature determines peak cell temperature
Generalizing hydrogen source from cathode reactions without evidence

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion cell with an NMC cathode and graphite anode uses an ionic liquid electrolyte with an inorganic anion. During a calorimetric abuse test, a small exothermic event is detected near 120°C, followed by a more violent heat release near 280°C. A separate cell using an organic anion (TFSI) ionic liquid electrolyte is tested under identical conditions. An engineer must assess which conclusions about the comparative thermal behavior of these two cells are admissible based on the described chemistry and observed reaction sequence.
options: A. The exothermic event near 120°C in both cells is consistent with reduction of the ionic liquid by the lithiated graphite anode following partial SEI film disruption.

B. The inorganic anion cell will exhibit a lower onset temperature for exothermic reactions and a larger total enthalpy release compared to the TFSI cell, making it thermally more hazardous in that respect.
C. Because the ionic liquid electrolyte is non-flammable, the violent heat release near 280°C is prevented, and the cell can be considered safe against thermal runaway.

D. The violent heat generation near 280°C is driven primarily by combustion reactions involving electrolyte and separator, fueled by oxygen released from the NMC cathode, not by the electrolyte flammability alone.
E. The lower CO2 generation from ionic liquid electrolytes compared to carbonate electrolytes directly implies that peak chamber pressure during thermal runaway will also be lower.
F. Replacing a carbonate electrolyte with an ionic liquid electrolyte eliminates the primary exothermic reaction sequence because the carbonate oxidation pathway is removed.
G. The onset temperature of the cathode-side oxidation reaction in the ionic liquid cell begins at a temperature substantially higher than the anode-side SEI-related event.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence explicitly states that small exothermic peaks near 120°C are ascribed to chemical reduction of ionic liquids by LixC6 anode when partial SEI film destruction occurs. This applies to ionic liquid cells generally, not just one anion type.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence states inorganic anions make onset temperatures lower and total enthalpies larger compared to organic anion (TFSI) electrolytes. This is a direct admissible comparative conclusion.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence explicitly states that cell safety does not directly connect to non-flammability of electrolytes based on the thermal runaway mechanism. Non-flammability does not prevent the violent heat release near 280°C, which is driven by cathode oxygen release and combustion of separator. This is a conservative-sounding but mechanistically incorrect claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence states the violent heat generation near 280°C is due to combustion reactions of electrolyte and separator based on released oxygen from the cathode. This is a multi-step mechanistic chain: cathode oxygen release → combustion of electrolyte and separator → violent heat, independent of bulk electrolyte flammability.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Lower CO2 generation from ionic liquid electrolytes is noted as a potential safety advantage, but the evidence on chamber pressure (from the heating method study) shows that gas generation rate, not CO2 fraction alone, drives peak pressure. Inferring lower peak pressure from lower CO2 generation is an unsupported cross-paper extrapolation and ignores other gas species contributions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence shows that ionic liquid cells still exhibit exothermic reactions at both anode and cathode sides. The carbonate structure is absent, reducing CO2, but the reaction sequence involving anode reduction and cathode oxidation still occurs. Replacing carbonate electrolyte does not eliminate the primary exothermic sequence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence places the anode-side SEI-related event at ~120°C and the cathode-side oxidation onset at ~220°C, with violent cathode-driven reactions at ~280°C. The cathode onset is substantially higher than the anode-side event, making this admissible.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanism-based admissibility of safety conclusions for ionic liquid electrolyte cells under thermal abuse, including the role of anion type, reaction sequence staging, and the decoupling of electrolyte flammability from cell safety.
analysis.primary_boundary: Non-flammability of ionic liquid electrolyte does not prevent thermal runaway; the violent heat release is driven by cathode oxygen release and combustion of separator, not bulk electrolyte flammability.
intended_error_modes: Protective mechanism overclaim: assuming non-flammable electrolyte prevents thermal runaway
Causal overclaim: inferring lower peak pressure from lower CO2 generation alone
Mechanism substitution: assuming removal of carbonate structure eliminates all exothermic reactions
disallowed_extrapolations: Do not infer that lower CO2 generation implies lower peak pressure without accounting for total gas generation rate
Do not generalize non-flammability as equivalent to thermal safety
Do not assume inorganic anion results apply to TFSI cells without comparison

solver_view.stem: A lithium-ion pouch cell is overcharged under adiabatic conditions. DSC analysis of the overcharged cathode shows no exothermic activity below 250°C. Separately, anode heat generation at 1.85 SOC exceeds 1.5 times its value at 1.00 SOC. Thermal runaway is observed at approximately 113°C in the baseline configuration. An engineer concludes that suppressing cathode decomposition is the primary intervention target to delay thermal runaway onset. Which statements are admissible given these conditions?
options: A. Cathode decomposition is not the dominant heat source driving thermal runaway onset below 150°C, because its exothermic activity begins well above the observed thermal runaway trigger temperature.
B. Increasing overcharge SOC monotonically reduces peak temperature during thermal runaway because higher lithium deposition increases internal resistance and limits current flow.
C. Anode-side reactions, not cathode decomposition, are the primary heat generation pathway accelerating temperature rise during overcharge thermal runaway.
D. Delaying separator melting to approximately 141°C via mechanical restraint constitutes a valid strategy to raise the thermal runaway trigger temperature under adiabatic overcharge.
E. Because cathode decomposition is negligible below 250°C, eliminating cathode side reactions entirely would prevent thermal runaway under all overcharge conditions.
F. The engineer's intervention target is misidentified; the evidence supports anode lithium deposition and separator failure as more mechanistically relevant triggers below 150°C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Cathode exothermic onset at ~250°C exceeds observed TTR of <150°C, making cathode decomposition mechanistically irrelevant to onset.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows Tmax increases with overcharge SOC; the proposed resistance-limiting mechanism is unsupported and contradicts observed trend.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Anode heat generation exceeds 1.5× at high SOC; cathode contributes negligibly below TTR, making anode the dominant pathway.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Configuration C raises TTR to ~141°C coinciding with PE separator melting, confirming mechanical restraint as a valid delay strategy.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Cathode elimination cannot prevent TR triggered by anode reactions and separator failure occurring well below 250°C.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly supports anode lithium deposition and separator melting as the operative triggers; cathode is ruled out below TTR.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Identifying the dominant heat source and trigger mechanism for overcharge thermal runaway below 150°C versus cathode decomposition onset temperature
analysis.primary_boundary: Cathode exothermic onset (~250°C) exceeds observed TTR (<150°C), making cathode decomposition inadmissible as a primary trigger; anode and separator mechanisms are operative in this regime
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming cathode suppression prevents all TR modes
Asserting resistance-limited current reduces Tmax at high SOC
Generalizing anode dominance beyond overcharge conditions

solver_view.stem: A parallel-connected 18650 NCM battery pack is subjected to a high-current overcharge event at approximately 3C rate. The pack includes cells both with and without current interrupt devices (CIDs). Engineers observe that CID-equipped cells interrupt current but several still progress to combustion, while the cell lacking a CID reaches thermal runaway significantly faster. Which conclusions about the role of CIDs under this condition are admissible?
options: A. The CID provides a meaningful delay to thermal runaway onset under high-rate overcharge, even though it cannot prevent the eventual escalation of internal side reactions.
B. The CID's current interruption function is sufficient to prevent thermal runaway at high charging rates, provided it activates before the battery voltage reaches the overcharge ceiling.
C. The absence of a CID eliminates the only available current interruption mechanism, causing earlier thermal runaway onset compared to CID-equipped cells under the same high-rate overcharge.
D. At high overcharge rates, the rate of internal heat and gas accumulation can outpace the protective response of the CID, rendering it unable to fully suppress thermal runaway.
E. Because CID-equipped cells still experience combustion at 3C, the CID provides no measurable protective benefit under any overcharge condition.
F. The probability of combustion at 3C overcharge rate is high enough that CID presence alone should not be treated as a sufficient safeguard against thermal runaway at that rate.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: CID-equipped cells outlasted the no-CID cell, confirming delay even without prevention under high-rate overcharge.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows CID-equipped cells still combusted at 3C; current interruption alone is insufficient to prevent thermal runaway at high rates.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Cell_7 without CID exploded at 424 s, significantly earlier than CID-equipped cells, confirming CID absence accelerates onset.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states internal reaction rate exceeds CID response speed under high-rate overcharge, preventing full suppression.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Overgeneralizes from high-rate failure; CID successfully prevented thermal runaway under low-current overcharge in all tested cells.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: 80% combustion probability at 3C with CID present supports that CID alone is an insufficient safeguard at that rate.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Regime-dependent effectiveness of CID protection under low vs. high overcharge current rates
analysis.primary_boundary: CID prevents thermal runaway under low-rate overcharge but cannot suppress it under high-rate overcharge due to reaction rate exceeding device response speed
disallowed_extrapolations: CID sufficiency at high rate
CID uselessness at all rates
voltage threshold as sole CID trigger criterion

solver_view.stem: A parallel-connected lithium-ion battery module undergoes thermal runaway propagation testing under varying discharge rates applied to the module prior to and during the propagation event. As discharge rate increases from 0 C to 3 C, the maximum temperature rate drops substantially, propagation time extends, and propagation rate declines by approximately three times. At 3 C discharge, batteries that previously produced visible flame instead emit only white smoke. Thermal conduction between adjacent cells accounts for 57–73% of inter-cell heat exchange throughout the propagation process regardless of discharge rate. An engineer evaluates several design and safety conclusions based on these observations. Which of the following conclusions are admissible?
options: A. Increasing discharge rate before and during a thermal runaway event reduces the stored electrochemical energy available to sustain propagation, which is mechanistically consistent with the observed reduction in temperature rate and propagation speed.

B. Because thermal conduction dominates inter-cell heat exchange at all tested discharge rates, eliminating direct cell-to-cell thermal contact would be sufficient to prevent propagation entirely under 3 C discharge conditions.

C. The transition from visible flame to white smoke emission at 3 C discharge indicates a change in the combustion or decomposition regime, but does not by itself confirm that propagation has been arrested.

D. The reduction in propagation rate at 3 C discharge to approximately one-third of the undischarged rate demonstrates that discharge rate is the only variable controlling propagation speed in this module configuration.

E. The observation that propagation time extends while propagation rate declines under higher discharge rates is internally consistent, since slower cell-to-cell spread necessarily requires more total time to traverse the same number of cells.

F. Since thermal conduction accounts for 57–73% of heat exchange between adjacent cells, radiation and convection together contribute the remaining fraction and cannot be neglected as secondary propagation pathways even at high discharge rates.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Discharging reduces stored electrochemical energy in the cells, which is the energy source for thermal runaway intensity. The evidence shows that higher discharge rate reduces maximum temperature rate and propagation speed, consistent with reduced available energy driving less intense and slower propagation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Even if thermal conduction is the dominant pathway (57–73%), the remaining 27–43% from radiation and convection is non-negligible. Eliminating conduction alone would not prevent propagation entirely; it would only reduce it. This is a protective mechanism overclaim distractor.
option_judgments.error_type: protective mechanism overclaim / ignoring secondary pathways

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence shows that at 3 C, cells emit white smoke instead of flame, indicating a change in the thermal decomposition or combustion character. However, the evidence also shows propagation still occurs at 3 C (propagation rate = 0.5 battery/min), so the absence of flame does not mean propagation is arrested.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence demonstrates discharge rate as an effective variable but does not establish it as the only controlling variable. Cell spacing, thermal conductivity, and module configuration are known system parameters affecting propagation. Claiming single-variable control is an unsupported overgeneralization.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: If propagation rate (batteries/min) declines, then traversing the same number of cells takes longer, so propagation time increases. This is internally consistent and directly supported by the evidence showing both trends simultaneously.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: If thermal conduction accounts for 57–73%, then radiation and convection together account for 27–43%, which is a substantial fraction. The evidence does not show this fraction diminishes with discharge rate, so these pathways remain relevant secondary contributors to propagation.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of safety and mechanistic conclusions about discharge-rate-mediated thermal runaway propagation mitigation in a parallel module
analysis.primary_boundary: Discharge rate reduces but does not eliminate propagation; dominant conduction pathway does not make secondary pathways negligible; flame suppression does not equal propagation arrest
intended_error_modes: Overclaiming that eliminating the dominant heat pathway prevents propagation entirely
Treating discharge rate as the sole controlling variable
Interpreting absence of visible flame as propagation arrest
disallowed_extrapolations: Eliminating conduction alone prevents propagation
Discharge rate is the only variable controlling propagation speed
White smoke emission confirms propagation has stopped

solver_view.stem: A prismatic cell module is being designed for two candidate chemistries: NMC-811 and LFP. During thermal runaway testing of individual cells, NMC-811 cells reach average peak temperatures of approximately 900°C and lose roughly 45% of their mass, while LFP cells reach approximately 524°C and lose roughly 21% of their mass. The NMC-811 thermal runaway reaction proceeds approximately nine times faster in energy terms than LFP. In the NMC-811 module, the time interval between successive cell thermal runaways increases exponentially, and this increase is localized entirely in the pause phase between events rather than in the internal reaction duration. In the LFP module, propagation velocity is five times lower overall. A structural engineer evaluating housing material selection and a safety engineer evaluating detection strategy must both make decisions based on these conditions. Which of the following conclusions are admissible?
options: A. The exponential increase in NMC-811 inter-cell propagation intervals is consistent with progressive increases in thermal contact resistance caused by mass ejection reducing mechanical clamping, rather than with any slowing of the internal thermal runaway reaction itself.

B. Because NMC-811 peak temperatures exceed the melting point of aluminum, using aluminum cell module housings for NMC-811 is inadmissible without additional thermal barriers, whereas aluminum remains structurally viable for LFP module housings under the same conditions.

C. The five times lower propagation velocity of LFP relative to NMC-811 implies that LFP cells individually generate less heat during thermal runaway, making them inherently safer at the cell level regardless of module configuration.

D. Voltage-based thermal runaway detection is less reliable for LFP than for NMC-811 in a slowly propagating parallel-connected configuration, because the voltage may remain within normal operating range and be indistinguishable from a high current load condition.

E. The higher mass loss in NMC-811 cells initially appears to accelerate propagation by increasing thermal contact, but the evidence indicates it actually progressively slows propagation by creating gaps that increase thermal contact resistance.
F. Because LFP venting gas does not ignite under the tested conditions, LFP battery packs require no venting path separation from high-voltage components.
G. The structural integrity difference between NMC-811 and LFP cells after thermal runaway is a contributing factor to the difference in their module-level propagation velocities.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence explicitly states that the exponential increase in inter-cell time span is found only in the pause time component, consistent with increasing thermal contact resistance from mass ejection reducing mechanical integrity and creating gaps. The internal reaction time does not show this trend.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: NMC-811 peak temperatures (~900°C) exceed aluminum's melting point (660°C), making aluminum structurally inadmissible for adjacent components without additional protection. LFP peak temperatures (~524°C) remain below this threshold, making aluminum viable for LFP housings. This is a direct evidence-supported boundary conclusion.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The five times lower module-level propagation velocity of LFP is a module-level outcome influenced by multiple factors including mass loss, contact resistance, and propagation dynamics—not a direct indicator that LFP cells generate less heat individually. This conflates module-level propagation velocity with cell-level heat generation, an inadmissible scale transfer.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence states that voltage-based detection is unreliable for slowly propagating chemistries or parallel-connected cells because voltage does not fully collapse and remains within normal operating range, making it indistinguishable from high current load. LFP's lower propagation velocity makes this limitation directly applicable.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: This is counterintuitive but evidence-supported: high mass loss in NMC-811 reduces mechanical integrity and creates gaps, increasing thermal contact resistance and slowing propagation in the pause phase. The initial expectation might be that mass loss accelerates contact, but the mechanism runs in the opposite direction.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: While LFP venting gas does not ignite under tested conditions, the evidence also states that venting gas temperatures exceed the melting points of common electrical insulation materials (PE, PTFE) for both chemistries, creating arcing risk. Concluding that no venting path separation is needed for LFP is an inadmissible safety overclaim that ignores the insulation melting risk.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LFP cells maintain mechanical integrity with ~21% mass loss, preserving thermal contact and consistent propagation, while NMC-811 cells lose ~45% mass, creating gaps and increasing thermal contact resistance that affects propagation timing. The evidence directly links structural integrity difference to propagation behavior difference.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-mechanism reasoning connecting mass loss, thermal contact resistance, propagation velocity, material selection boundaries, and detection reliability across NMC-811 and LFP chemistries
analysis.primary_boundary: Module-level propagation velocity cannot be directly equated with cell-level heat generation; mass loss counterintuitively slows NMC-811 propagation via contact resistance; LFP venting gas still poses insulation melting risk despite non-ignition
intended_error_modes: scale_transfer: inferring cell-level heat generation from module-level propagation velocity
protective_mechanism_overclaim: concluding no venting separation needed because LFP gas does not ignite
mechanism_confusion: expecting mass loss to accelerate rather than slow propagation
disallowed_extrapolations: Lower propagation velocity implies lower cell-level heat generation
Non-igniting venting gas eliminates all high-voltage insulation risks
Pause time increase reflects accelerating internal cell reaction

solver_view.stem: A prismatic lithium-ion cell undergoes 0.5C overcharge under a 3000 N preload. During the early overcharge stage, the surface temperature stays below 50°C and the voltage is relatively stable, yet the internal expansion force rises substantially. At safety valve activation, strain hardening and strain rate hardening together exceed thermal softening by more than 350%. Which conclusions about casing mechanical state at valve opening are admissible?
options: A. The casing is mechanically strengthened relative to its pre-overcharge state because hardening mechanisms dominate thermal softening at this stage.
B. The low surface temperature at valve opening confirms that no significant exothermic electrochemical reactions have occurred inside the cell.
C. Thermal softening is the governing failure mechanism at valve opening because the temperature has already risen enough to weaken the casing.
D. The casing has accumulated plastic strain before valve opening, evidenced by the large expansion force, yet retains elevated yield strength due to strain hardening dominance.
E. The large expansion force rise indicates that internal gas pressure alone determines casing yield strength at this stage.
F. Casing integrity at valve opening cannot be assessed without knowing whether the corner region has exceeded its plastic strain limit.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states hardening exceeds thermal softening by 371.59% at valve opening, so net mechanical state is strengthened.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Low surface temperature does not exclude internal reactions; lithium plating and electrolyte decomposition drive force rise at this stage.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Thermal softening governs only after thermal runaway, not at valve opening where hardening effects dominate by 371.59%.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Force rise from ~3163 N to ~13919 N implies plastic deformation; yet hardening dominance means yield strength is elevated, not reduced.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Yield strength is determined by the balance of strain hardening, strain rate hardening, and thermal softening, not gas pressure alone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence quantifies the net mechanical state at valve opening directly; corner strain data refines but does not preclude the admissible conclusion.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanical state of cell casing at safety valve activation during overcharge: hardening vs. softening dominance regime
analysis.primary_boundary: Hardening-dominance regime at valve opening versus thermal-softening-dominance regime after thermal runaway
intended_error_modes: Confusing post-TR softening regime with pre-TR valve-opening regime
Treating low surface temperature as proof of no internal reactions
Attributing yield strength change to gas pressure rather than material hardening mechanisms
disallowed_extrapolations: Concluding thermal softening governs at valve opening based on post-TR evidence
Inferring internal electrochemical state from surface temperature alone

solver_view.stem: A process safety analyst is comparing LFP and NMC pouch cell thermal runaway behavior under thermal abuse at SOC = 100%. LFP cells reach maximum temperatures of 278–286°C and do not ignite regardless of SOC. NMC cells reach 505–548°C under thermal abuse and 585–669°C under electrical abuse at the same SOC. The analyst must classify which conclusions about chemistry-dependent fire risk and abuse-mode sensitivity are defensible.
options: A. The absence of ignition in LFP cells across all SOC levels under thermal abuse suggests that LFP pouch cells do not produce sufficient heat or reactive gas to support combustion under those conditions.

B. The higher peak temperature of NMC cells under electrical abuse compared to thermal abuse at the same SOC indicates that the exothermic reactions triggered by overcharging are more intense than those triggered by external heating alone.
C. Because LFP cells do not ignite during thermal runaway, they can be considered safe for use in enclosed spaces without additional fire suppression measures.

D. The temperature gap between LFP and NMC thermal runaway peaks at SOC = 100% is large enough that a single fixed temperature threshold for fire detection would perform differently for the two chemistries.

E. The difference in NMC peak temperatures between thermal and electrical abuse modes implies that abuse mode must be considered when sizing thermal management or suppression systems for NMC installations.
F. LFP cells reaching lower peak temperatures than NMC cells under identical abuse conditions proves that LFP chemistry has a fundamentally lower energy density.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Non-ignition is observed but attributing it specifically to insufficient heat or reactive gas is a mechanistic overclaim not supported by the evidence; absence of sparks is cited as the cause.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: NMC electrical abuse temperatures (585–669°C) exceed thermal abuse temperatures (505–548°C) at the same SOC, directly supporting greater exothermic intensity under electrical abuse.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Non-ignition under thermal abuse does not eliminate all hazards in enclosed spaces; gas venting and pressure effects remain unaddressed by this claim.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: LFP peaks at 278–286°C versus NMC at 505–548°C represent a ~220°C gap; a single fixed threshold optimized for one chemistry would be poorly matched to the other.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: An ~80–120°C difference in NMC peak temperature between abuse modes at the same SOC is a direct engineering input for sizing thermal management and suppression systems.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Peak thermal runaway temperature reflects reaction kinetics and thermal stability, not electrochemical energy density; conflating these two properties is a mechanism confusion error.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissible fire risk and system design conclusions from chemistry-dependent and abuse-mode-dependent peak temperature differences
analysis.primary_boundary: Peak temperature differences support detection threshold and suppression sizing conclusions but do not imply energy density relationships or unconditional safety
intended_error_modes: system-level safety overclaim from component non-ignition
mechanism confusion between peak TR temperature and energy density
disallowed_extrapolations: LFP non-ignition implies no hazard in enclosed spaces
peak TR temperature as proxy for electrochemical energy density
single abuse-mode result generalizing to all abuse modes

solver_view.stem: A researcher is characterizing thermal runaway gas emissions from three cell types: NMC811/SiC 21700 lithium-ion (LIB), NMC811/SiC 18650 lithium-ion (LIB), and NaMn1/3Fe1/3Ni1/3O2 18650 sodium-ion (SIB). All cells are subjected to the same heating rate. Key observations: LIB venting gas temperatures exceeded 1000°C and in some cases reached the thermocouple measurement limit of 1370°C, while the actual temperature may have been higher. SIB peak venting gas temperatures did not exceed 350°C, and no gas ignition was observed. Average total mass loss was 77% for 21700 LIBs, 75% for 18650 LIBs, and 55% for 18650 SIBs. The SIB thermal runaway onset temperature was 10–15°C higher than for the 18650 LIBs. Optical velocity estimation of venting gas was not possible for the SIBs. A second engineer argues that the SIBs are categorically safer than LIBs under all thermal abuse conditions based on these results. Evaluate the following statements.
options: A. The inability to optically estimate venting gas velocity for SIBs is a direct consequence of the absence of gas ignition, meaning the measurement methodology itself is contingent on a phenomenon that represents a primary hazard in LIBs.

B. The lower total mass loss in SIBs compared to LIBs, combined with the absence of gas ignition, is consistent with a less energetic thermal runaway event, but does not by itself establish that the SIB venting gas composition is non-hazardous.
C. Because peak venting gas temperatures for LIBs reached the thermocouple measurement limit, the reported LIB venting gas temperatures represent confirmed upper bounds on the actual temperatures.

D. The 10–15°C higher thermal runaway onset temperature of SIBs compared to LIBs, under the same heating rate, indicates that SIBs have a larger thermal margin before runaway initiation, but this margin does not directly characterize the severity of the runaway event once initiated.

E. The peak mass flow rate values of 2000 g/s for 18650 LIBs and 1500 g/s for 21700 LIBs, despite lasting only milliseconds, imply that the 18650 format poses a greater sustained mechanical pressure hazard than the 21700 format.

F. The second engineer's conclusion that SIBs are categorically safer under all thermal abuse conditions is not fully supported, because the available data characterizes only a specific SIB chemistry and format under a single heating protocol.

G. The venting gas velocity of LIBs exceeding 100 m/s at the onset of thermal runaway and decaying below 50 m/s after 200 ms is consistent with a transient, ignition-driven gas release rather than a sustained steady-state flow.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence explicitly states that optical velocity estimation was impossible for SIBs because the vented gas did not ignite. The optical method depends on visible ignition, which is itself the primary fire hazard in LIBs. This is a correct reasoning chain: absence of ignition → measurement limitation, and ignition = primary LIB hazard.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lower mass loss and no ignition are consistent with lower energy release, but the evidence does not characterize SIB venting gas composition. Gas toxicity or reactivity hazards cannot be excluded from the available data. This is a correct admissibility boundary judgment.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence states the thermocouple reached its measurement limit of 1370°C and that actual temperatures may have been higher. Therefore, 1370°C is a lower bound on actual temperature, not a confirmed upper bound. This option inverts the measurement limitation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The 10–15°C higher onset temperature indicates a larger thermal margin before initiation under the tested heating rate. However, onset temperature and post-onset severity are distinct parameters. The evidence does not link higher onset temperature to reduced severity once runaway begins.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence explicitly states these peak values last only milliseconds. A millisecond-duration peak does not constitute a sustained mechanical pressure hazard. Inferring sustained hazard from a transient peak is a scope expansion that ignores the duration qualifier.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The data covers one SIB chemistry in 18650 format under one heating protocol. Generalizing to all thermal abuse conditions and all SIB chemistries is an unsupported extrapolation. This is a correct admissibility boundary judgment about the scope of the experimental evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence shows LIB venting gas velocity exceeding 100 m/s at TR onset, attributed to gas ignition, then decaying below 50 m/s after 200 ms and below 25 m/s after 500 ms. This temporal decay pattern is consistent with a transient ignition-driven event, not a steady-state flow.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of comparative safety conclusions between LIBs and SIBs based on venting gas characterization data, including measurement limitations, scope boundaries, and the distinction between onset conditions and runaway severity.
analysis.primary_boundary: The thermocouple limit establishes a lower bound, not an upper bound, on LIB venting temperature; peak mass flow rate duration must be considered before inferring sustained hazard; SIB safety conclusions are bounded to the tested chemistry, format, and protocol.
intended_error_modes: measurement_scope_inversion: treating thermocouple limit as upper bound rather than lower bound
scope_expansion_ignoring_duration: inferring sustained hazard from millisecond-duration peak
overgeneralization: extending single-chemistry, single-protocol SIB results to all conditions
disallowed_extrapolations: Treating 1370°C as a confirmed maximum LIB venting temperature
Inferring sustained pressure hazard from millisecond peak mass flow rate
Concluding SIBs are categorically safer under all conditions from one chemistry and protocol

solver_view.stem: A researcher performs thermogravimetric analysis coupled with infrared spectroscopy on extracted LiPF6-based electrolyte containing both EC and DMC solvents. The heating rate is controlled and the carrier gas contains trace residual moisture. Solvent vapor signals appear in two distinct temperature ranges. Fluorine-containing gas signals appear only after 100°C and both HF and POF3 signals decline near 170°C. No PF5 signal is detected throughout. Which of the following mechanistic interpretations are consistent with these observations?
options: A. The two-stage solvent vapor signal is consistent with DMC evaporating in the lower temperature range and EC evaporating in the higher temperature range, reflecting their different volatility characteristics.
B. The absence of PF5 signal confirms that LiPF6 does not decompose via any pathway involving PF5 as an intermediate, since a stable intermediate would necessarily be detectable.

C. The appearance of both HF and POF3 above 100°C is consistent with LiPF6 decomposition initiating in that temperature range, with POF3 acting as an intermediate that partially converts to HF via reaction with residual moisture.

D. The decline of HF signal near 170°C, occurring simultaneously with POF3 decline, is more consistent with moisture depletion limiting further POF3-to-HF conversion than with complete cessation of LiPF6 decomposition.
E. Because HF and POF3 appear simultaneously, the data confirm that HF is produced exclusively through the POF3 intermediate route and not through any direct LiPF6 decomposition pathway.
F. The trace residual moisture in the carrier gas is sufficient to explain partial POF3-to-HF conversion, consistent with both species being detected rather than only POF3.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states non-cyclic carbonates (including DMC) evaporate below 100°C and cyclic carbonates (EC) above 100°C, directly explaining the two-stage signal.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explains PF5 absence by its short lifetime and reactivity, not by its absence from the decomposition pathway; non-detection does not rule out transient intermediacy.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence supports LiPF6 decomposition producing both HF and POF3 from 100°C, with POF3 serving as HF precursor via moisture reaction.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly attributes the simultaneous decline near 170°C to insufficient residual humidity for POF3-to-HF conversion, not to decomposition completion.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Simultaneous appearance is consistent with POF3 being a precursor but does not exclude direct HF production; the evidence does not establish exclusivity of the POF3 route.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states residual humidity was insufficient to fully convert POF3 to HF, implying partial conversion did occur, which is consistent with detecting both species.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Mechanistic interpretation of TG/FTIR gas emission patterns from LiPF6 electrolyte thermal decomposition including solvent volatility staging and fluorine species dynamics
analysis.primary_boundary: PF5 non-detection reflects reactivity not pathway absence; simultaneous HF/POF3 appearance does not establish exclusive production route; decline at 170°C reflects humidity depletion not decomposition completion
intended_error_modes: inferring pathway absence from non-detection of reactive intermediate
claiming exclusive HF production route from co-detection pattern
treating signal decline as decomposition completion
disallowed_extrapolations: Ruling out PF5 intermediacy based on non-detection
Claiming HF is produced exclusively via POF3 route
Concluding LiPF6 decomposition ends at 170°C

solver_view.stem: A researcher uses an accelerated rate calorimetry method to study thermal runaway in 18650-type NCM111 cells at varying states of charge between 50% and 120% SOC. Three characteristic temperatures are identified: T1 (self-heating onset), T2 (separator collapse), and T3 (maximum temperature during thermal runaway). Peak pressure P3 is also recorded. The following trends are observed: T1 increases as SOC decreases; T3 decreases as SOC decreases; P3 varies more strongly with SOC than T3 does. A kinetic model is trained on this dataset using a neural-network-assisted method. The researcher proposes using this model to predict thermal runaway behavior in a prismatic 50 Ah NCM811 cell for a vehicle application. Which of the following statements about the experimental findings and the proposed model application are admissible?
options: A. The stronger variation of P3 with SOC compared to T3 indicates that the total amount of gas released during thermal runaway is more sensitive to SOC than the peak temperature reached.
B. Because T1 increases monotonically as SOC decreases from 120% to 50%, a cell at 50% SOC can be considered thermally safe under all operating conditions without further thermal management.
C. The fact that T3 decreases with decreasing SOC is consistent with the cell being less reactive and having suppressed exothermic reactions at lower charge states.

D. Applying the kinetic parameters from this NCM111 18650 study directly to predict thermal runaway onset in a 50 Ah NCM811 prismatic cell is not supported without additional validation data for that cell format and chemistry.

E. The lower activation energies associated with the violent thermal runaway stage reactions, compared to self-heating stage reactions, imply that once the violent stage is initiated, it proceeds more slowly and is easier to interrupt.
F. The monotonic increase in T1 with decreasing SOC means that reducing SOC is always sufficient to prevent thermal runaway initiation in NCM cells under any abuse condition.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence directly states that variations in P3 between cells with different SOCs are much more obvious than those of T3, indicating that total released gas amount differs greatly with SOC. This conclusion is admissible and directly supported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: While T1 is higher at lower SOC, this means the self-heating onset is delayed, not that the cell is thermally safe under all conditions. The evidence does not support a claim of unconditional safety at 50% SOC. This is an inadmissible overgeneralization from a threshold comparison.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states that T3 decreases with decreased SOC and that this indicates the cell is less reactive with suppressed exothermic reactions at lower SOC. This conclusion is directly supported.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states that the kinetic parameters are primarily applicable to 18650-type NCM111 cells and that incorporating more experimental data is essential to extend applicability to cells with different capacities and cathode materials. Applying these parameters to NCM811 prismatic cells without additional data is not supported.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states that lower activation energies in the violent TR stage cause those reactions to proceed at a faster rate, not slower. This option inverts the correct causal relationship between activation energy and reaction rate, making it directly contradictory to the evidence.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence shows T1 increases monotonically with decreasing SOC within the 50–120% range studied, but does not support the claim that SOC reduction alone is always sufficient to prevent thermal runaway under any abuse condition. This is an inadmissible universal claim that ignores other abuse pathways and the conditional nature of the evidence.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of conclusions about SOC-dependent thermal runaway parameters and the scope limitations of kinetic models trained on specific cell formats and chemistries
analysis.primary_boundary: Scope limitation boundary: kinetic parameters derived from NCM111 18650 cells cannot be directly transferred to different capacities and cathode materials without additional data
intended_error_modes: Inverting the activation energy-reaction rate relationship (E)
Overgeneralizing monotonic T1 trend to unconditional safety at low SOC (B and F)
Ignoring explicit model applicability limitations when proposing cross-chemistry transfer (D)
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming low SOC guarantees thermal safety under all abuse conditions
Applying NCM111 18650 kinetic parameters to NCM811 prismatic cells without validation
Inferring slow reaction rate from low activation energy

solver_view.stem: A safety engineer compares gas venting hazard across four SOC levels (25%, 50%, 75%, 100%) for the same cell type under identical external heating. The FED for asphyxiant gases at 25% SOC is 0.002, rising to 0.033 at 100% SOC. The maximum venting rate at 25% SOC is 0.9 m/s versus 7.2 m/s at 100% SOC. The 25% SOC cell did not exhibit violent venting. The engineer proposes that operating cells below 50% SOC eliminates meaningful asphyxiant risk in open environments. Which evaluation of this proposal is most defensible?
options: A. The proposal is supported because the FED at 25% SOC is 0.002, which is negligible, and the cell did not exhibit violent venting, confirming that low-SOC operation removes both mechanical and toxic gas hazards.

B. The proposal overstates the conclusion: while FED values at low SOC are substantially lower, the evidence characterizes open-condition cell-level measurements and does not establish that enclosed or poorly ventilated environments would remain below harmful thresholds.
C. The proposal is undermined because the FED increase from 25% to 50% SOC is already an order of magnitude, suggesting that even 50% SOC cannot be considered low-risk for asphyxiant exposure.
D. The proposal is partially defensible for mechanical venting hazard reduction but is not admissible as a claim about asphyxiant safety without specifying the enclosure volume and ventilation rate.
E. The proposal is fully defensible because all FED values across all SOC levels remain below 1.0, which is the threshold for incapacitation.
F. The absence of violent venting at 25% SOC confirms that no toxic gases are generated at that SOC, supporting the engineer's proposal.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The low FED and absence of violent venting at 25% SOC are cell-level open-condition results; concluding both mechanical and toxic hazards are eliminated in all environments overgeneralizes the evidence scope.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: FED measurements are cell-level open-condition values; enclosure effects on gas accumulation are not captured, making the proposal inadmissible as a general asphyxiant safety claim.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: FED rises from 0.002 to 0.022 between 25% and 50% SOC, which is about an order of magnitude, but this does not by itself make 50% SOC high-risk; the absolute values remain very low in open conditions.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Venting rate reduction with lower SOC supports reduced mechanical hazard, but asphyxiant safety requires enclosure and ventilation context not provided by cell-level FED data alone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: All cell-level FED values being below 1.0 does not guarantee safety in enclosed environments where gases can accumulate beyond cell-level concentrations.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Absence of violent venting does not mean zero toxic gas generation; it means insufficient energy to drive violent venting. Some gas release may still occur at low SOC.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of a low-SOC operational safety proposal for asphyxiant and mechanical venting hazard
analysis.primary_boundary: Cell-level open-condition FED values cannot be directly applied to enclosed environments; mechanical and asphyxiant hazard reductions are separable claims with different evidence bases
disallowed_extrapolations: FED below 1.0 guarantees safe asphyxiant conditions in all environments
Absence of violent venting implies absence of toxic gas generation

solver_view.stem: A safety engineer compares thermal runaway characteristics of LFP and NCM811 battery chemistries for use in an enclosed battery pack. Both chemistries undergo thermal runaway under identical test conditions. Gas composition analysis shows LFP produces gas with 50.82% H2 and 9.3% CO, while NCM811 produces gas with lower H2 content. Normalized gas production measurements show NCM811 produces 2.1 L/Ah while LFP produces 0.569 L/Ah. The engineer must determine which safety-related conclusions are admissible based on these measurements.
options: A: LFP presents lower overall hazard than NCM811 because its normalized gas production volume is approximately one-fourth that of NCM811.
B: The lower flammability limit of the LFP gas mixture is lower than that of NCM811 due to the higher H2 fraction in LFP gas, despite LFP producing less total gas volume.
C: From a gas volume perspective, NCM811 poses greater hazard, but from a flammability composition perspective, LFP poses greater hazard due to its lower flammability limit.
D: The higher H2 content in LFP gas indicates that LFP thermal runaway is more energetic than NCM811 thermal runaway on a per-cell basis.
E: Because LFP produces significantly less gas volume, the risk of pressure-driven cell-to-cell propagation in the enclosed pack is lower for LFP than for NCM811.
F: The thermal stability ranking based on trigger temperature is consistent with the hazard ranking based on gas production volume.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: This conclusion considers only gas volume and ignores gas composition. Evidence shows that while LFP produces less gas volume, its gas has a lower flammability limit due to high H2 content, reversing the hazard ranking when composition is considered.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence states LFP gas contains 50.82% H2 compared to lower H2 in NCM, and that the higher H2 content results in a lower LFL due to the relatively low LFL of H2 itself.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence explicitly states that based on gas production volume, NCM811 poses greater harm than LFP, but from the perspective of gas composition (flammability), the hazard ranking reverses with LFP > NCM811.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Higher H2 content in the vented gas does not necessarily indicate more energetic thermal runaway. H2 content reflects decomposition pathways and gas composition, not total energy release. Evidence does not support inferring energy release from gas composition alone.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: While LFP produces less gas volume, the evidence does not establish a direct relationship between normalized gas production and cell-to-cell propagation risk in an enclosed pack. Propagation depends on multiple factors including heat transfer, not just gas volume.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows LFP has higher trigger temperature (~184°C) than NCM811 (~130-140°C), indicating better thermal stability for LFP. However, the gas volume hazard ranking is NCM811 > LFP. These rankings are consistent. But the flammability composition hazard ranking is LFP > NCM811, which is inconsistent with thermal stability ranking. The statement is too broad and ignores the composition-based hazard reversal.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Multi-factor hazard assessment requiring integration of gas volume and gas composition effects
analysis.primary_boundary: Distinction between volume-based and composition-based hazard metrics, which produce opposite rankings
intended_error_modes: single-factor reasoning (considering only volume or only composition)
inferring energy release from gas composition
extrapolating from gas volume to propagation risk without supporting mechanism
assuming consistency between thermal stability and all hazard metrics
disallowed_extrapolations: inferring cell-to-cell propagation behavior from normalized gas production alone
inferring thermal runaway energy from gas composition

solver_view.stem: A seven-cell lithium-ion mini-module undergoes nail penetration of a single cell under two conditions: (1) air-cooled baseline and (2) active dielectric immersion cooling at 2 LPM and 25°C inlet temperature. In the baseline case, all cell thermocouples exceed 1000°C within 20 seconds and module voltage drops to 0 V within seconds. In the immersion-cooled case, peak temperatures remain below 400°C near the penetrated cell, most surface temperatures return below 100°C within 60–90 seconds, and two non-penetrated cells retain voltages above 4.1 V post-test. However, coolant flow rate drops to near zero approximately two minutes after penetration due to bulk vaporization of the dielectric fluid. An engineer must evaluate which conclusions about the immersion cooling strategy are admissible based on this outcome. Which of the following conclusions are supported?
options: A. The immersion cooling system suppressed thermal runaway propagation to the majority of non-penetrated cells, as evidenced by retained cell voltages and the approximately tenfold reduction in module mass loss.
B. The immersion cooling system eliminated the thermal runaway event in the penetrated cell, because peak temperatures remained below the threshold associated with full thermal runaway.

C. The loss of coolant flow at approximately two minutes represents a failure of the cooling system's sustained protection capability, meaning the system cannot be relied upon to maintain thermal control beyond that point without fluid replenishment.
D. The temperature reduction achieved by immersion cooling was primarily due to increased thermal mass of the module rather than active heat removal by the circulating fluid.

E. The retention of functional voltage in two of seven cells after the test is consistent with partial propagation arrest, but does not confirm that those cells experienced no electrochemical degradation.

F. Because silicone oil provides a larger thermal runaway onset delay than fluorinated fluids in single-cell overcharge tests, silicone oil would have produced superior propagation suppression in this nail penetration scenario.

G. The approximately 24-gram mass loss in the immersion-cooled case versus more than 260 grams in the baseline case indicates that the immersion fluid substantially limited the ejection or combustion of cell materials beyond the initially penetrated cell.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Two of seven cells retained near-nominal voltages (4.1 V and 4.2 V), and module mass loss was approximately 24.4 g versus more than 260 g in the baseline. These jointly support the conclusion that propagation to the majority of non-penetrated cells was suppressed. The tenfold characterization is a minimal arithmetic inference from the stated values.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The penetrated cell (Cell #1) was the initiation site and thermocouples near it exceeded 200°C; the evidence does not state that the penetrated cell itself avoided thermal runaway, only that propagation to most neighbors was limited. Concluding that the penetrated cell avoided thermal runaway is an unsupported overclaim.
option_judgments.error_type: Causal overclaim; scope expansion beyond stated evidence

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The evidence explicitly states that coolant flow dropped to near zero after two minutes due to bulk vaporization. This is a documented loss of active cooling capability. Concluding that sustained protection beyond this point requires fluid replenishment is a minimal and directly supported engineering implication.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The evidence explicitly attributes the rapid heat dissipation to the continuously circulating dielectric fluid, not to thermal mass. This option substitutes a different mechanism (thermal mass) for the one supported by the evidence (active convective removal by circulating fluid).

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Retained voltage is a macroscopic indicator of cell integrity but does not directly measure internal electrochemical state. The conclusion that propagation was partially arrested is supported, but inferring zero degradation in those cells exceeds what voltage measurement alone can confirm. This is a measurement scope limitation.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: The silicone oil onset delay data comes from single-cell overcharge tests. The nail penetration scenario involves a different abuse mode, different heat generation pathway, and critically, silicone oil was shown to produce strong explosions and severe flames in overcharge tests with LTO cells, indicating it is not universally superior. Transferring the onset delay ranking from overcharge to nail penetration is an unsupported regime transfer.
option_judgments.error_type: Regime transfer beyond stated conditions; scope expansion

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: The approximately tenfold difference in mass loss (24.4 g vs. more than 260 g) directly supports the conclusion that the immersion fluid substantially limited material ejection or combustion beyond the penetrated cell. This is a direct minimal inference from the stated mass loss comparison.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Admissibility of conclusions about immersion cooling effectiveness and its limitations under nail penetration abuse, including mechanism attribution and measurement scope
analysis.primary_boundary: Immersion cooling demonstrably suppressed propagation but did not eliminate the initiating cell's thermal event, and its active cooling capability was time-limited by coolant vaporization
intended_error_modes: Mechanism substitution (thermal mass vs. active convection)
Regime transfer from overcharge to nail penetration for fluid ranking
Overclaiming elimination of thermal runaway in the penetrated cell
Treating voltage retention as proof of zero internal degradation
disallowed_extrapolations: Inferring that the penetrated cell avoided thermal runaway because neighbors were protected
Transferring single-cell overcharge fluid rankings to nail penetration scenarios
Claiming sustained cooling beyond coolant vaporization without replenishment

solver_view.stem: A simulation study of a lithium-ion battery pack applies external heating at 5000 W/m² from different positions. The safety ranking by time-to-failure is: vertical 10 mm > bottom end > bottom 5 mm > centre 10 mm > bottom 10 mm. Separately, increasing heating power from 7000 to 10,000 W/m² reduces time delay by 17% but raises maximum temperature by only 10 K less at the higher power. A thermal engineer claims that maximizing heating power always minimizes peak temperature because faster runaway reduces cumulative heat accumulation time.
options: A. The engineer's claim is unsupported; higher heating power reduces time to thermal runaway but does not guarantee lower peak temperature, as the 7000 W/m² case reaches a higher maximum temperature than 10,000 W/m².
B. Heating position affects time-to-failure more than heating power magnitude, because the safety ranking across positions spans multiple configurations at fixed power.
C. The counterintuitive result that lower power produces higher peak temperature is consistent with longer dwell time allowing more complete internal reaction progression.
D. Because bottom 10 mm is the least safe heating position, relocating the heat source to vertical 10 mm will prevent thermal runaway under all power levels.
E. The 17% reduction in time delay from increasing power is sufficient evidence to recommend 10,000 W/m² as the safer operating condition in all pack geometries.

F. Heating position and power level interact to determine thermal runaway characteristics; conclusions about safety ranking at one power level cannot be directly transferred to a different power regime without additional evidence.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Evidence shows 7000 W/m² produces 10 K higher Tmax than 10,000 W/m², directly contradicting the engineer's claim.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Evidence provides position ranking at fixed power but does not establish a comparative magnitude claim against power effects; this is an unsupported scope expansion.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Lower power extends time to TR, allowing more complete reaction progression and higher Tmax; mechanistically consistent with dwell-time-dependent heat release.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Position ranking is established at 5000 W/m²; claiming prevention of TR at all power levels is an inadmissible generalization beyond stated conditions.

option_judgments.label: Incorrect
option_judgments.justification: Time delay reduction does not establish safety; higher power causes earlier TR and the peak temperature relationship is non-monotonic, not a universal safety recommendation.

option_judgments.label: Correct
option_judgments.justification: Position and power interact; safety ranking at 5000 W/m² cannot be assumed to hold at 7000 or 10,000 W/m² without supporting evidence.

analysis.target_cognitive_object: Non-monotonic relationship between heating power and peak temperature, and the inadmissibility of generalizing position-based safety rankings across power regimes
analysis.primary_boundary: Higher power reduces time to TR but does not reduce peak temperature; safety rankings derived at one power level are not transferable to other power levels without evidence
disallowed_extrapolations: Claiming position ranking holds across all power levels
Asserting higher power is universally safer
Inferring prevention from position optimization alone

solver_view.stem: A simulation study of thermal runaway propagation in a battery pack compares a full model (including both natural convection and thermal radiation) against two reduced models: one omitting natural convection and one omitting thermal radiation. The omission of natural convection produces a peak temperature that is 150 K lower and a time-to-peak that is 52 seconds earlier compared to the full model. The omission of thermal radiation produces a peak temperature that is 52.4 K lower and a time-to-peak that is 125 seconds earlier. In a separate set of simulations, the cell-to-cell gap is varied. At zero gap, natural convection does not alter the sequence in which cells enter thermal runaway. At nonzero gaps, larger gaps increase the time interval before the last cell enters thermal runaway. An engineer concludes that for pack-level thermal runaway propagation modeling, radiation is the more important heat transfer mechanism to include accurately because omitting it causes a larger time-to-peak error. Which of the following evaluations of this conclusion and the underlying evidence are correct?
options: A. The engineer's conclusion is not supported: omitting natural convection causes a larger peak temperature error (150 K) than omitting radiation (52.4 K), indicating that natural convection has a greater influence on peak temperature prediction accuracy.

B. The engineer's conclusion is partially supported for time-to-peak accuracy but does not extend to peak temperature accuracy, because the two omissions produce errors of opposite relative magnitude depending on which metric is considered.

C. At zero cell-to-cell gap, including or excluding natural convection in the model does not affect the predicted sequence of cell triggering, which is consistent with the absence of airflow in that configuration.
